Roofs are either {{nihongo|gabled|切妻造|[[Gabled roof|kirizuma-zukuri]]}} or {{nihongo|hip-and-gable|入母屋造|[[irimoya-zukuri]]}}. ==Gallery== Chion-in bell 1 by MShades in Kyoto.jpg|Great Bell at Chion-in Gousyouzi 11.jpg|Goshoji-Temple Horyuji-L0337.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Sai-in Shōrō, an example of Nara period bell tower Horyu-ji37s3200.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō, a typical ''hakamagoshi'' type Takidani-hudoumyououji-syourou.jpg|Takidani-hudōmyōō-ji Shōrō Todaiji shoro.jpg|Tōdai-ji's bell tower, an example of the ''fukihanachi'' type, although much larger than the average Komyoji01s3200.jpg|Sometimes the bell is installed in the ''[[rōmon]]''. 崇福寺 (岐阜市)-鐘楼soufukuji007.jpg| Taipei City Tone-Wa Tample Bell tower.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. 臨濟護國禪寺舊鐘樓山門.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]] ==References== {{commons Category|Bell towers in Japan}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shoro}} [[Category:Japanese architectural features]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist architecture]] [[Category:Bell towers]] [[Category:Shinto architecture]] [[Category:Traditional East Asian Architecture]] [[en:Shōrō]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:qq 4]] [[en:Shōrō]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:qq 4]] [[en:Shōrō]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Shōrō]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:qq 4]] [[en:Shōrō]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Shōrō]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Shōrō]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[en:Shōrō]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shōrō]][[Category:qq]]

{{Short description|Bell towers in Japan}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:kk]][[Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}}
{| style="border-collapse:collapse" cellpadding="0" |style="border:1px solid black;"|[[File:Keiunji Au10 08.JPG|alt=Wooden belltower, old style|x200px]] |style="border:1px solid black;"|[[File:Kencho-ji,-Bell.jpg|alt=Wooden belltower, new style|x200px]] |}
The two main types of bell tower in Japan
The {{nihongo|'''''shōrō''''', '''''shurō'''''|鐘楼|lit. bell building}} or {{nihongo|'''''kanetsuki-[[Dō (architecture)|dō]]'''''|鐘突堂|lit. bell-striking hall}} is the [[bell tower]] of a [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple in Japan]], housing the temple's {{nihongo|''[[bonshō]]''|梵鐘}}. It can also be found at some [[Shinto shrine]]s which used to function as temples (see article ''[[Shinbutsu shūgō]]''), as for example [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]. Two main types exist, the older {{nihongo|''hakamagoshi''|袴腰}}, which has walls, and the more recent {{nihongo|''fukihanachi''|吹放ち}} or {{nihongo|''fukinuki''|吹貫・吹抜き}}, which does not.{{cite book | title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi | editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku | publisher=Shōwa-dō | date=April 10, 1990 | edition=September 30, 2008 | isbn=4-8122-9805-9 | page=82|language=ja}} ==History== During the [[Nara period]] (710–794), immediately after the arrival of [[Buddhism in Japan]] bell towers were 3 x 2 [[Ken (architecture)|bay]], 2 storied buildings.{{cite web|title=Shourou|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shourou.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=22 September 2010}} A typical temple ''[[garan]]'' had normally two, one to the left and one to the right of the ''[[kyōzō]]'' (or ''kyō-dō''), the [[sūtra]] repository. An extant example of this style is [[Hōryū-ji]]'s Sai-in Shōrō in [[Nara, Nara|Nara]] (see photo in the gallery). During the following [[Heian period]] (794–1185) was developed a new style called ''hakamagoshi'' which consisted of a two storied, [[:en:Hourglass|hourglass]]-shaped building with the bell hanging from the second story. The earliest extant example is Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō (see photo in the gallery). Finally, during the 13th century the ''fukihanachi'' type was created at [[Tōdai-ji]] by making all structural parts visible. The bell tower in this case usually consists of a 1-ken wide, 1-ken high structure with no walls and having the bell at its center (see photo above). Sometimes the four pillars have an inward inclination called {{nihongo|uchikorobi|内転び|lit. inward fall}}. After the Nara period, in which temple layout was rigidly prescribed after the Chinese fashion, the position of the bell tower stopped being prescribed and began to change temple by temple. Roofs are either {{nihongo|gabled|切妻造|[[Gabled roof|kirizuma-zukuri]]}} or {{nihongo|hip-and-gable|入母屋造|[[irimoya-zukuri]]}}. ==Gallery== Chion-in bell 1 by MShades in Kyoto.jpg|Great Bell at Chion-in Gousyouzi 11.jpg|Goshoji-Temple Horyuji-L0337.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Sai-in Shōrō, an example of Nara period bell tower Horyu-ji37s3200.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō, a typical ''hakamagoshi'' type Takidani-hudoumyououji-syourou.jpg|Takidani-hudōmyōō-ji Shōrō Todaiji shoro.jpg|Tōdai-ji's bell tower, an example of the ''fukihanachi'' type, although much larger than the average Komyoji01s3200.jpg|Sometimes the bell is installed in the ''[[rōmon]]''. 崇福寺 (岐阜市)-鐘楼soufukuji007.jpg| Taipei City Tone-Wa Tample Bell tower.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. 臨濟護國禪寺舊鐘樓山門.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]] ==References== {{commons Category|Bell towers in Japan}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shoro}} [[Category:Japanese architectural features]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist architecture]] [[Category:Bell towers]] [[Category:Shinto architecture]] [[Category:Traditional East Asian Architecture]] [[en:Shōrō]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:qq 4]] [[en:Shōrō]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:qq 4]] [[en:Shōrō]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Shōrō]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:qq 4]] [[en:Shōrō]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Shōrō]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Shōrō]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[en:Shōrō]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shōrō]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]]

Roofs are either {{nihongo|gabled|切妻造|[[Gabled roof|kirizuma-zukuri]]}} or {{nihongo|hip-and-gable|入母屋造|[[irimoya-zukuri]]}}. ==Gallery== Chion-in bell 1 by MShades in Kyoto.jpg|Great Bell at Chion-in Gousyouzi 11.jpg|Goshoji-Temple Horyuji-L0337.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Sai-in Shōrō, an example of Nara period bell tower Horyu-ji37s3200.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō, a typical ''hakamagoshi'' type Takidani-hudoumyououji-syourou.jpg|Takidani-hudōmyōō-ji Shōrō Todaiji shoro.jpg|Tōdai-ji's bell tower, an example of the ''fukihanachi'' type, although much larger than the average Komyoji01s3200.jpg|Sometimes the bell is installed in the ''[[rōmon]]''. 崇福寺 (岐阜市)-鐘楼soufukuji007.jpg| Taipei City Tone-Wa Tample Bell tower.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. 臨濟護國禪寺舊鐘樓山門.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]] ==References== {{commons Category|Bell towers in Japan}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shoro}} [[Category:Japanese architectural features]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist architecture]] [[Category:Bell towers]] [[Category:Shinto architecture]] [[Category:Traditional East Asian Architecture]] [[en:Shōrō]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:11 4]] [[en:Shōrō]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:11 4]] [[en:Shōrō]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Shōrō]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:11 4]] [[en:Shōrō]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:11]][[Category:Shōrō]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[Category:Shōrō]][[Category:11]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[en:Shōrō]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Shōrō]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]]

{{Short description|Bell towers in Japan}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:kk]][[Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}}
{| style="border-collapse:collapse" cellpadding="0" |style="border:1px solid black;"|[[File:Keiunji Au10 08.JPG|alt=Wooden belltower, old style|x200px]] |style="border:1px solid black;"|[[File:Kencho-ji,-Bell.jpg|alt=Wooden belltower, new style|x200px]] |}
The two main types of bell tower in Japan
The {{nihongo|'''''shōrō''''', '''''shurō'''''|鐘楼|lit. bell building}} or {{nihongo|'''''kanetsuki-[[Dō (architecture)|dō]]'''''|鐘突堂|lit. bell-striking hall}} is the [[bell tower]] of a [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple in Japan]], housing the temple's {{nihongo|''[[bonshō]]''|梵鐘}}. It can also be found at some [[Shinto shrine]]s which used to function as temples (see article ''[[Shinbutsu shūgō]]''), as for example [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]. Two main types exist, the older {{nihongo|''hakamagoshi''|袴腰}}, which has walls, and the more recent {{nihongo|''fukihanachi''|吹放ち}} or {{nihongo|''fukinuki''|吹貫・吹抜き}}, which does not.{{cite book | title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi | editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku | publisher=Shōwa-dō | date=April 10, 1990 | edition=September 30, 2008 | isbn=4-8122-9805-9 | page=82|language=ja}} ==History== During the [[Nara period]] (710–794), immediately after the arrival of [[Buddhism in Japan]] bell towers were 3 x 2 [[Ken (architecture)|bay]], 2 storied buildings.{{cite web|title=Shourou|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shourou.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=22 September 2010}} A typical temple ''[[garan]]'' had normally two, one to the left and one to the right of the ''[[kyōzō]]'' (or ''kyō-dō''), the [[sūtra]] repository. An extant example of this style is [[Hōryū-ji]]'s Sai-in Shōrō in [[Nara, Nara|Nara]] (see photo in the gallery). During the following [[Heian period]] (794–1185) was developed a new style called ''hakamagoshi'' which consisted of a two storied, [[:en:Hourglass|hourglass]]-shaped building with the bell hanging from the second story. The earliest extant example is Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō (see photo in the gallery). Finally, during the 13th century the ''fukihanachi'' type was created at [[Tōdai-ji]] by making all structural parts visible. The bell tower in this case usually consists of a 1-ken wide, 1-ken high structure with no walls and having the bell at its center (see photo above). Sometimes the four pillars have an inward inclination called {{nihongo|uchikorobi|内転び|lit. inward fall}}. After the Nara period, in which temple layout was rigidly prescribed after the Chinese fashion, the position of the bell tower stopped being prescribed and began to change temple by temple. Roofs are either {{nihongo|gabled|切妻造|[[Gabled roof|kirizuma-zukuri]]}} or {{nihongo|hip-and-gable|入母屋造|[[irimoya-zukuri]]}}. ==Gallery== Chion-in bell 1 by MShades in Kyoto.jpg|Great Bell at Chion-in Gousyouzi 11.jpg|Goshoji-Temple Horyuji-L0337.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Sai-in Shōrō, an example of Nara period bell tower Horyu-ji37s3200.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō, a typical ''hakamagoshi'' type Takidani-hudoumyououji-syourou.jpg|Takidani-hudōmyōō-ji Shōrō Todaiji shoro.jpg|Tōdai-ji's bell tower, an example of the ''fukihanachi'' type, although much larger than the average Komyoji01s3200.jpg|Sometimes the bell is installed in the ''[[rōmon]]''. 崇福寺 (岐阜市)-鐘楼soufukuji007.jpg| Taipei City Tone-Wa Tample Bell tower.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. 臨濟護國禪寺舊鐘樓山門.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]] ==References== {{commons Category|Bell towers in Japan}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shoro}} [[Category:Japanese architectural features]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist architecture]] [[Category:Bell towers]] [[Category:Shinto architecture]] [[Category:Traditional East Asian Architecture]] [[en:Shōrō]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:11 4]] [[en:Shōrō]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:11 4]] [[en:Shōrō]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Shōrō]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:11 4]] [[en:Shōrō]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:11]][[Category:Shōrō]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[Category:Shōrō]][[Category:11]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[en:Shōrō]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Shōrō]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]]

{{Short description|Bell towers in Japan}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:kk]]}}

The two main types of bell tower in Japan
The {{nihongo|'''''shōrō''''', '''''shurō'''''|鐘楼|lit. bell building}} or {{nihongo|'''''kanetsuki-[[Dō (architecture)|dō]]'''''|鐘突堂|lit. bell-striking hall}} is the [[bell tower]] of a [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple in Japan]], housing the temple's {{nihongo|''[[bonshō]]''|梵鐘}}. It can also be found at some [[Shinto shrine]]s which used to function as temples (see article ''[[Shinbutsu shūgō]]''), as for example [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]. Two main types exist, the older {{nihongo|''hakamagoshi''|袴腰}}, which has walls, and the more recent {{nihongo|''fukihanachi''|吹放ち}} or {{nihongo|''fukinuki''|吹貫・吹抜き}}, which does not.{{cite book | title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi | editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku | publisher=Shōwa-dō | date=April 10, 1990 | edition=September 30, 2008 | isbn=4-8122-9805-9 | page=82|language=ja}} ==History== During the [[Nara period]] (710–794), immediately after the arrival of [[Buddhism in Japan]] bell towers were 3 x 2 [[Ken (architecture)|bay]], 2 storied buildings.{{cite web|title=Shourou|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shourou.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=22 September 2010}} A typical temple ''[[garan]]'' had normally two, one to the left and one to the right of the ''[[kyōzō]]'' (or ''kyō-dō''), the [[sūtra]] repository. An extant example of this style is [[Hōryū-ji]]'s Sai-in Shōrō in [[Nara, Nara|Nara]] (see photo in the gallery). During the following [[Heian period]] (794–1185) was developed a new style called ''hakamagoshi'' which consisted of a two storied, [[:en:Hourglass|hourglass]]-shaped building with the bell hanging from the second story. The earliest extant example is Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō (see photo in the gallery). Finally, during the 13th century the ''fukihanachi'' type was created at [[Tōdai-ji]] by making all structural parts visible. The bell tower in this case usually consists of a 1-ken wide, 1-ken high structure with no walls and having the bell at its center (see photo above). Sometimes the four pillars have an inward inclination called {{nihongo|uchikorobi|内転び|lit. inward fall}}. After the Nara period, in which temple layout was rigidly prescribed after the Chinese fashion, the position of the bell tower stopped being prescribed and began to change temple by temple. Roofs are either {{nihongo|gabled|切妻造|[[Gabled roof|kirizuma-zukuri]]}} or {{nihongo|hip-and-gable|入母屋造|[[irimoya-zukuri]]}}. ==Gallery== Chion-in bell 1 by MShades in Kyoto.jpg|Great Bell at Chion-in Gousyouzi 11.jpg|Goshoji-Temple Horyuji-L0337.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Sai-in Shōrō, an example of Nara period bell tower Horyu-ji37s3200.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō, a typical ''hakamagoshi'' type Takidani-hudoumyououji-syourou.jpg|Takidani-hudōmyōō-ji Shōrō Todaiji shoro.jpg|Tōdai-ji's bell tower, an example of the ''fukihanachi'' type, although much larger than the average Komyoji01s3200.jpg|Sometimes the bell is installed in the ''[[rōmon]]''. 崇福寺 (岐阜市)-鐘楼soufukuji007.jpg| Taipei City Tone-Wa Tample Bell tower.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. 臨濟護國禪寺舊鐘樓山門.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]] ==References== {{commons Category|Bell towers in Japan}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{Shinto shrine}} ]] ]] ]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Japanese architectural features]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist architecture]] [[Category:Bell towers]] [[Category:Shinto architecture]] [[Category:Traditional East Asian Architecture]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]] [[Category:11 4]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Shōrō]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[en:Shōrō]] [[sn:Shōrō]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Shoro}}

Two main types exist, the older {{nihongo|''hakamagoshi''|袴腰}}, which has walls, and the more recent {{nihongo|''fukihanachi''|吹放ち}} or {{nihongo|''fukinuki''|吹貫・吹抜き}}, which does not.{{cite book | title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi | editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku | publisher=Shōwa-dō | date=April 10, 1990 | edition=September 30, 2008 | isbn=4-8122-9805-9 | page=82|language=ja}} ==History== During the [[Nara period]] (710–794), immediately after the arrival of [[Buddhism in Japan]] bell towers were 3 x 2 [[Ken (architecture)|bay]], 2 storied buildings.{{cite web|title=Shourou|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shourou.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=22 September 2010}} A typical temple ''[[garan]]'' had normally two, one to the left and one to the right of the ''[[kyōzō]]'' (or ''kyō-dō''), the [[sūtra]] repository. An extant example of this style is [[Hōryū-ji]]'s Sai-in Shōrō in [[Nara, Nara|Nara]] (see photo in the gallery). During the following [[Heian period]] (794–1185) was developed a new style called ''hakamagoshi'' which consisted of a two storied, [[:en:Hourglass|hourglass]]-shaped building with the bell hanging from the second story. The earliest extant example is Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō (see photo in the gallery). Finally, during the 13th century the ''fukihanachi'' type was created at [[Tōdai-ji]] by making all structural parts visible. The bell tower in this case usually consists of a 1-ken wide, 1-ken high structure with no walls and having the bell at its center (see photo above). Sometimes the four pillars have an inward inclination called {{nihongo|uchikorobi|内転び|lit. inward fall}}. After the Nara period, in which temple layout was rigidly prescribed after the Chinese fashion, the position of the bell tower stopped being prescribed and began to change temple by temple. Roofs are either {{nihongo|gabled|切妻造|[[Gabled roof|kirizuma-zukuri]]}} or {{nihongo|hip-and-gable|入母屋造|[[irimoya-zukuri]]}}. ==Gallery== Chion-in bell 1 by MShades in Kyoto.jpg|Great Bell at Chion-in Gousyouzi 11.jpg|Goshoji-Temple Horyuji-L0337.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Sai-in Shōrō, an example of Nara period bell tower Horyu-ji37s3200.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō, a typical ''hakamagoshi'' type Takidani-hudoumyououji-syourou.jpg|Takidani-hudōmyōō-ji Shōrō Todaiji shoro.jpg|Tōdai-ji's bell tower, an example of the ''fukihanachi'' type, although much larger than the average Komyoji01s3200.jpg|Sometimes the bell is installed in the ''[[rōmon]]''. 崇福寺 (岐阜市)-鐘楼soufukuji007.jpg| Taipei City Tone-Wa Tample Bell tower.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. 臨濟護國禪寺舊鐘樓山門.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]] ==References== {{commons Category|Bell towers in Japan}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Japanese architectural features]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist architecture]] [[Category:Bell towers]] [[Category:Shinto architecture]] [[Category:Traditional East Asian Architecture]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Shōrō]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[en:Shōrō]] [[sn:Shōrō]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Shoro}} [[Category:New]]

{{Short description|Bell towers in Japan}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:kk]]}}
{| style="border-collapse:collapse" cellpadding="0" |style="border:1px solid black;"|[[File:Keiunji Au10 08.JPG|alt=Wooden belltower, old style|x200px]] |style="border:1px solid black;"|[[File:Kencho-ji,-Bell.jpg|alt=Wooden belltower, new style|x200px]] |}
The two main types of bell tower in Japan
The {{nihongo|'''''shōrō''''', '''''shurō'''''|鐘楼|lit. bell building}} or {{nihongo|'''''kanetsuki-[[Dō (architecture)|dō]]'''''|鐘突堂|lit. bell-striking hall}} is the [[bell tower]] of a [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple in Japan]], housing the temple's {{nihongo|''[[bonshō]]''|梵鐘}}. It can also be found at some [[Shinto shrine]]s which used to function as temples (see article ''[[Shinbutsu shūgō]]''), as for example [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]. Two main types exist, the older {{nihongo|''hakamagoshi''|袴腰}}, which has walls, and the more recent {{nihongo|''fukihanachi''|吹放ち}} or {{nihongo|''fukinuki''|吹貫・吹抜き}}, which does not.{{cite book | title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi | editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku | publisher=Shōwa-dō | date=April 10, 1990 | edition=September 30, 2008 | isbn=4-8122-9805-9 | page=82|language=ja}} ==History== During the [[Nara period]] (710–794), immediately after the arrival of [[Buddhism in Japan]] bell towers were 3 x 2 [[Ken (architecture)|bay]], 2 storied buildings.{{cite web|title=Shourou|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shourou.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=22 September 2010}} A typical temple ''[[garan]]'' had normally two, one to the left and one to the right of the ''[[kyōzō]]'' (or ''kyō-dō''), the [[sūtra]] repository. An extant example of this style is [[Hōryū-ji]]'s Sai-in Shōrō in [[Nara, Nara|Nara]] (see photo in the gallery). During the following [[Heian period]] (794–1185) was developed a new style called ''hakamagoshi'' which consisted of a two storied, [[:en:Hourglass|hourglass]]-shaped building with the bell hanging from the second story. The earliest extant example is Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō (see photo in the gallery). Finally, during the 13th century the ''fukihanachi'' type was created at [[Tōdai-ji]] by making all structural parts visible. The bell tower in this case usually consists of a 1-ken wide, 1-ken high structure with no walls and having the bell at its center (see photo above). Sometimes the four pillars have an inward inclination called {{nihongo|uchikorobi|内転び|lit. inward fall}}. After the Nara period, in which temple layout was rigidly prescribed after the Chinese fashion, the position of the bell tower stopped being prescribed and began to change temple by temple. Roofs are either {{nihongo|gabled|切妻造|[[Gabled roof|kirizuma-zukuri]]}} or {{nihongo|hip-and-gable|入母屋造|[[irimoya-zukuri]]}}. ==Gallery== Chion-in bell 1 by MShades in Kyoto.jpg|Great Bell at Chion-in Gousyouzi 11.jpg|Goshoji-Temple Horyuji-L0337.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Sai-in Shōrō, an example of Nara period bell tower Horyu-ji37s3200.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō, a typical ''hakamagoshi'' type Takidani-hudoumyououji-syourou.jpg|Takidani-hudōmyōō-ji Shōrō Todaiji shoro.jpg|Tōdai-ji's bell tower, an example of the ''fukihanachi'' type, although much larger than the average Komyoji01s3200.jpg|Sometimes the bell is installed in the ''[[rōmon]]''. 崇福寺 (岐阜市)-鐘楼soufukuji007.jpg| Taipei City Tone-Wa Tample Bell tower.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. 臨濟護國禪寺舊鐘樓山門.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]] ==References== {{commons Category|Bell towers in Japan}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Japanese architectural features]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist architecture]] [[Category:Bell towers]] [[Category:Shinto architecture]] [[Category:Traditional East Asian Architecture]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Shōrō]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[en:Shōrō]] [[sn:Shōrō]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Shoro}}

Roofs are either {{nihongo|gabled|切妻造|[[Gabled roof|kirizuma-zukuri]]}} or {{nihongo|hip-and-gable|入母屋造|[[irimoya-zukuri]]}}. ==Gallery== Chion-in bell 1 by MShades in Kyoto.jpg|Great Bell at Chion-in Gousyouzi 11.jpg|Goshoji-Temple Horyuji-L0337.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Sai-in Shōrō, an example of Nara period bell tower Horyu-ji37s3200.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō, a typical ''hakamagoshi'' type Takidani-hudoumyououji-syourou.jpg|Takidani-hudōmyōō-ji Shōrō Todaiji shoro.jpg|Tōdai-ji's bell tower, an example of the ''fukihanachi'' type, although much larger than the average Komyoji01s3200.jpg|Sometimes the bell is installed in the ''[[rōmon]]''. 崇福寺 (岐阜市)-鐘楼soufukuji007.jpg| Taipei City Tone-Wa Tample Bell tower.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. 臨濟護國禪寺舊鐘樓山門.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]] ==References== {{commons Category|Bell towers in Japan}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Japanese architectural features]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist architecture]] [[Category:Bell towers]] [[Category:Shinto architecture]] [[Category:Traditional East Asian Architecture]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Shōrō]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[en:Shōrō]] [[sn:Shōrō]]

{{Short description|Bell towers in Japan}} {{Italic title|reason=}}
{| style="border-collapse:collapse" cellpadding="0" |style="border:1px solid black;"|[[File:Keiunji Au10 08.JPG|alt=Wooden belltower, old style|x200px]] |style="border:1px solid black;"|[[File:Kencho-ji,-Bell.jpg|alt=Wooden belltower, new style|x200px]] |}
The two main types of bell tower in Japan
The {{nihongo|'''''shōrō''''', '''''shurō'''''|鐘楼|lit. bell building}} or {{nihongo|'''''kanetsuki-[[Dō (architecture)|dō]]'''''|鐘突堂|lit. bell-striking hall}} is the [[bell tower]] of a [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple in Japan]], housing the temple's {{nihongo|''[[bonshō]]''|梵鐘}}. It can also be found at some [[Shinto shrine]]s which used to function as temples (see article ''[[Shinbutsu shūgō]]''), as for example [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]. Two main types exist, the older {{nihongo|''hakamagoshi''|袴腰}}, which has walls, and the more recent {{nihongo|''fukihanachi''|吹放ち}} or {{nihongo|''fukinuki''|吹貫・吹抜き}}, which does not.{{cite book | title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi | editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku | publisher=Shōwa-dō | date=April 10, 1990 | edition=September 30, 2008 | isbn=4-8122-9805-9 | page=82|language=ja}} ==History== During the [[Nara period]] (710–794), immediately after the arrival of [[Buddhism in Japan]] bell towers were 3 x 2 [[Ken (architecture)|bay]], 2 storied buildings.{{cite web|title=Shourou|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shourou.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=22 September 2010}} A typical temple ''[[garan]]'' had normally two, one to the left and one to the right of the ''[[kyōzō]]'' (or ''kyō-dō''), the [[sūtra]] repository. An extant example of this style is [[Hōryū-ji]]'s Sai-in Shōrō in [[Nara, Nara|Nara]] (see photo in the gallery). During the following [[Heian period]] (794–1185) was developed a new style called ''hakamagoshi'' which consisted of a two storied, [[:en:Hourglass|hourglass]]-shaped building with the bell hanging from the second story. The earliest extant example is Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō (see photo in the gallery). Finally, during the 13th century the ''fukihanachi'' type was created at [[Tōdai-ji]] by making all structural parts visible. The bell tower in this case usually consists of a 1-ken wide, 1-ken high structure with no walls and having the bell at its center (see photo above). Sometimes the four pillars have an inward inclination called {{nihongo|uchikorobi|内転び|lit. inward fall}}. After the Nara period, in which temple layout was rigidly prescribed after the Chinese fashion, the position of the bell tower stopped being prescribed and began to change temple by temple. Roofs are either {{nihongo|gabled|切妻造|[[Gabled roof|kirizuma-zukuri]]}} or {{nihongo|hip-and-gable|入母屋造|[[irimoya-zukuri]]}}. ==Gallery== Chion-in bell 1 by MShades in Kyoto.jpg|Great Bell at Chion-in Gousyouzi 11.jpg|Goshoji-Temple Horyuji-L0337.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Sai-in Shōrō, an example of Nara period bell tower Horyu-ji37s3200.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō, a typical ''hakamagoshi'' type Takidani-hudoumyououji-syourou.jpg|Takidani-hudōmyōō-ji Shōrō Todaiji shoro.jpg|Tōdai-ji's bell tower, an example of the ''fukihanachi'' type, although much larger than the average Komyoji01s3200.jpg|Sometimes the bell is installed in the ''[[rōmon]]''. 崇福寺 (岐阜市)-鐘楼soufukuji007.jpg| Taipei City Tone-Wa Tample Bell tower.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. 臨濟護國禪寺舊鐘樓山門.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]] ==References== {{commons Category|Bell towers in Japan}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Japanese architectural features]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist architecture]] [[Category:Bell towers]] [[Category:Shinto architecture]] [[Category:Traditional East Asian Architecture]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]] [[Category:Shōrō]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[en:Shōrō]] [[sn:Shōrō]]

An extant example of this style is [[Hōryū-ji]]'s Sai-in Shōrō in [[Nara, Nara|Nara]] (see photo in the gallery). During the following [[Heian period]] (794–1185) was developed a new style called ''hakamagoshi'' which consisted of a two storied, [[:en:Hourglass|hourglass]]-shaped building with the bell hanging from the second story. The earliest extant example is Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō (see photo in the gallery). Finally, during the 13th century the ''fukihanachi'' type was created at [[Tōdai-ji]] by making all structural parts visible. The bell tower in this case usually consists of a 1-ken wide, 1-ken high structure with no walls and having the bell at its center (see photo above). Sometimes the four pillars have an inward inclination called {{nihongo|uchikorobi|内転び|lit. inward fall}}. After the Nara period, in which temple layout was rigidly prescribed after the Chinese fashion, the position of the bell tower stopped being prescribed and began to change temple by temple. Roofs are either {{nihongo|gabled|切妻造|[[Gabled roof|kirizuma-zukuri]]}} or {{nihongo|hip-and-gable|入母屋造|[[irimoya-zukuri]]}}. ==Gallery== Chion-in bell 1 by MShades in Kyoto.jpg|Great Bell at Chion-in Gousyouzi 11.jpg|Goshoji-Temple Horyuji-L0337.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Sai-in Shōrō, an example of Nara period bell tower Horyu-ji37s3200.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō, a typical ''hakamagoshi'' type Takidani-hudoumyououji-syourou.jpg|Takidani-hudōmyōō-ji Shōrō Todaiji shoro.jpg|Tōdai-ji's bell tower, an example of the ''fukihanachi'' type, although much larger than the average Komyoji01s3200.jpg|Sometimes the bell is installed in the ''[[rōmon]]''. 崇福寺 (岐阜市)-鐘楼soufukuji007.jpg| Taipei City Tone-Wa Tample Bell tower.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. 臨濟護國禪寺舊鐘樓山門.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]] ==References== {{commons Category|Bell towers in Japan}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Japanese architectural features]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist architecture]] [[Category:Bell towers]] [[Category:Shinto architecture]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[en:Shōrō]] [[sn:Shōrō]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]]

Roofs are either {{nihongo|gabled|切妻造|[[:en:Gabled roof|kirizuma-zukuri]]}} or {{nihongo|hip-and-gable|入母屋造|[[irimoya-zukuri]]}}. ==Gallery== Chion-in bell 1 by MShades in Kyoto.jpg|Great Bell at Chion-in Gousyouzi 11.jpg|Goshoji-Temple Horyuji-L0337.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Sai-in Shōrō, an example of Nara period bell tower Horyu-ji37s3200.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō, a typical ''hakamagoshi'' type Takidani-hudoumyououji-syourou.jpg|Takidani-hudōmyōō-ji Shōrō Todaiji shoro.jpg|Tōdai-ji's bell tower, an example of the ''fukihanachi'' type, although much larger than the average Komyoji01s3200.jpg|Sometimes the bell is installed in the ''[[rōmon]]''. 崇福寺 (岐阜市)-鐘楼soufukuji007.jpg| Taipei City Tone-Wa Tample Bell tower.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. 臨濟護國禪寺舊鐘樓山門.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]] ==References== {{commons Category|Bell towers in Japan}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Japanese architectural features]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist architecture]] [[Category:Bell towers]] [[Category:Shinto architecture]] }} [[en:Shōrō]] [[sn:Shōrō]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]] {{Commons category|Bell towers in Japan}} [[de:Shōrō]] [[es:Shōrō]] [[eu:Shōrō]] [[fr:Shōrō]] [[id:Shōrō]] [[it:Shōrō]] [[ja:鐘楼]] [[wuu:钟楼（东亚）]] [[zh:鐘樓 (東亞)]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Bell towers in Japan}} {{Italic title|reason=}}
{| style="border-collapse:collapse" cellpadding="0" |style="border:1px solid black;"|[[File:Keiunji Au10 08.JPG|alt=Wooden belltower, old style|x200px]] |style="border:1px solid black;"|[[File:Kencho-ji,-Bell.jpg|alt=Wooden belltower, new style|x200px]] |}
The two main types of bell tower in Japan
The {{nihongo|'''''shōrō''''', '''''shurō'''''|鐘楼|lit. bell building}} or {{nihongo|'''''kanetsuki-[[Dō (architecture)|dō]]'''''|鐘突堂|lit. bell-striking hall}} is the [[:en:Bell tower|Bell tower]] of a [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple in Japan]], housing the temple's {{nihongo|''[[:en:Bonshō|Bonshō]]''|梵鐘}}. It can also be found at some [[Shinto shrine]]s which used to function as temples (see article ''[[Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu shūgō]]''), as for example [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]. Two main types exist, the older {{nihongo|''hakamagoshi''|袴腰}}, which has walls, and the more recent {{nihongo|''fukihanachi''|吹放ち}} or {{nihongo|''fukinuki''|吹貫・吹抜き}}, which does not.{{cite book | title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi | editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku | publisher=Shōwa-dō | date=April 10, 1990 | edition=September 30, 2008 | isbn=4-8122-9805-9 | page=82|language=ja}} ==History== During the [[Nara period]] (710–794), immediately after the arrival of [[Buddhism in Japan]] bell towers were 3 x 2 [[Ken (architecture)|bay]], 2 storied buildings.{{cite web|title=Shourou|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shourou.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=22 September 2010}} A typical temple ''[[garan]]'' had normally two, one to the left and one to the right of the ''[[kyōzō]]'' (or ''kyō-dō''), the [[sūtra]] repository. An extant example of this style is [[Hōryū-ji]]'s Sai-in Shōrō in [[Nara, Nara|Nara]] (see photo in the gallery). During the following [[Heian period]] (794–1185) was developed a new style called ''hakamagoshi'' which consisted of a two storied, [[:en:Hourglass|hourglass]]-shaped building with the bell hanging from the second story. The earliest extant example is Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō (see photo in the gallery). Finally, during the 13th century the ''fukihanachi'' type was created at [[Tōdai-ji]] by making all structural parts visible. The bell tower in this case usually consists of a 1-ken wide, 1-ken high structure with no walls and having the bell at its center (see photo above). Sometimes the four pillars have an inward inclination called {{nihongo|uchikorobi|内転び|lit. inward fall}}. After the Nara period, in which temple layout was rigidly prescribed after the Chinese fashion, the position of the bell tower stopped being prescribed and began to change temple by temple. Roofs are either {{nihongo|gabled|切妻造|[[:en:Gabled roof|kirizuma-zukuri]]}} or {{nihongo|hip-and-gable|入母屋造|[[irimoya-zukuri]]}}. ==Gallery== Chion-in bell 1 by MShades in Kyoto.jpg|Great Bell at Chion-in Gousyouzi 11.jpg|Goshoji-Temple Horyuji-L0337.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Sai-in Shōrō, an example of Nara period bell tower Horyu-ji37s3200.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō, a typical ''hakamagoshi'' type Takidani-hudoumyououji-syourou.jpg|Takidani-hudōmyōō-ji Shōrō Todaiji shoro.jpg|Tōdai-ji's bell tower, an example of the ''fukihanachi'' type, although much larger than the average Komyoji01s3200.jpg|Sometimes the bell is installed in the ''[[rōmon]]''. 崇福寺 (岐阜市)-鐘楼soufukuji007.jpg| Taipei City Tone-Wa Tample Bell tower.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. 臨濟護國禪寺舊鐘樓山門.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]] ==References== {{commons Category|Bell towers in Japan}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Japanese architectural features]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist architecture]] [[Category:Bell towers]] [[Category:Shinto architecture]] }} [[en:Shōrō]] [[sn:Shōrō]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis 2]] {{Commons category|Bell towers in Japan}} [[de:Shōrō]] [[es:Shōrō]] [[eu:Shōrō]] [[fr:Shōrō]] [[id:Shōrō]] [[it:Shōrō]] [[ja:鐘楼]] [[wuu:钟楼（东亚）]] [[zh:鐘樓 (東亞)]]

Roofs are either {{nihongo|gabled|切妻造|[[:en:Gabled roof|kirizuma-zukuri]]}} or {{nihongo|hip-and-gable|入母屋造|[[irimoya-zukuri]]}}. ==Gallery== Chion-in bell 1 by MShades in Kyoto.jpg|Great Bell at Chion-in Gousyouzi 11.jpg|Goshoji-Temple Horyuji-L0337.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Sai-in Shōrō, an example of Nara period bell tower Horyu-ji37s3200.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō, a typical ''hakamagoshi'' type Takidani-hudoumyououji-syourou.jpg|Takidani-hudōmyōō-ji Shōrō Todaiji shoro.jpg|Tōdai-ji's bell tower, an example of the ''fukihanachi'' type, although much larger than the average Komyoji01s3200.jpg|Sometimes the bell is installed in the ''[[rōmon]]''. 崇福寺 (岐阜市)-鐘楼soufukuji007.jpg| Taipei City Tone-Wa Tample Bell tower.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. 臨濟護國禪寺舊鐘樓山門.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]] ==References== {{commons Category|Bell towers in Japan}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Japanese architectural features]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist architecture]] [[Category:Bell towers]] [[Category:Shinto architecture]] }} [[en:Shōrō]] [[sn:Shōrō]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] {{Commons category|Bell towers in Japan}} [[de:Shōrō]] [[es:Shōrō]] [[eu:Shōrō]] [[fr:Shōrō]] [[id:Shōrō]] [[it:Shōrō]] [[ja:鐘楼]] [[wuu:钟楼（东亚）]] [[zh:鐘樓 (東亞)]]

{{Short description|Bell towers in Japan}} {{Italic title|reason=}}
{| style="border-collapse:collapse" cellpadding="0" |style="border:1px solid black;"|[[File:Keiunji Au10 08.JPG|alt=Wooden belltower, old style|x200px]] |style="border:1px solid black;"|[[File:Kencho-ji,-Bell.jpg|alt=Wooden belltower, new style|x200px]] |}
The two main types of bell tower in Japan
The {{nihongo|'''''shōrō''''', '''''shurō'''''|鐘楼|lit. bell building}} or {{nihongo|'''''kanetsuki-[[Dō (architecture)|dō]]'''''|鐘突堂|lit. bell-striking hall}} is the [[:en:Bell tower|Bell tower]] of a [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple in Japan]], housing the temple's {{nihongo|''[[:en:Bonshō|Bonshō]]''|梵鐘}}. It can also be found at some [[Shinto shrine]]s which used to function as temples (see article ''[[Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu shūgō]]''), as for example [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]. Two main types exist, the older {{nihongo|''hakamagoshi''|袴腰}}, which has walls, and the more recent {{nihongo|''fukihanachi''|吹放ち}} or {{nihongo|''fukinuki''|吹貫・吹抜き}}, which does not.{{cite book | title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi | editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku | publisher=Shōwa-dō | date=April 10, 1990 | edition=September 30, 2008 | isbn=4-8122-9805-9 | page=82|language=ja}} ==History== During the [[Nara period]] (710–794), immediately after the arrival of [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism in Japan]] bell towers were 3 x 2 [[Ken (architecture)|bay]], 2 storied buildings.{{cite web|title=Shourou|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shourou.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=22 September 2010}} A typical temple ''[[:en:Garan|Garan]]'' had normally two, one to the left and one to the right of the ''[[kyōzō]]'' (or ''kyō-dō''), the [[sūtra]] repository. An extant example of this style is [[Hōryū-ji]]'s Sai-in Shōrō in [[Nara, Nara|Nara]] (see photo in the gallery). During the following [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] (794–1185) was developed a new style called ''hakamagoshi'' which consisted of a two storied, [[:en:Hourglass|hourglass]]-shaped building with the bell hanging from the second story. The earliest extant example is Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō (see photo in the gallery). Finally, during the 13th century the ''fukihanachi'' type was created at [[Tōdai-ji]] by making all structural parts visible. The bell tower in this case usually consists of a 1-ken wide, 1-ken high structure with no walls and having the bell at its center (see photo above). Sometimes the four pillars have an inward inclination called {{nihongo|uchikorobi|内転び|lit. inward fall}}. After the Nara period, in which temple layout was rigidly prescribed after the Chinese fashion, the position of the bell tower stopped being prescribed and began to change temple by temple. Roofs are either {{nihongo|gabled|切妻造|[[:en:Gabled roof|kirizuma-zukuri]]}} or {{nihongo|hip-and-gable|入母屋造|[[irimoya-zukuri]]}}. ==Gallery== Chion-in bell 1 by MShades in Kyoto.jpg|Great Bell at Chion-in Gousyouzi 11.jpg|Goshoji-Temple Horyuji-L0337.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Sai-in Shōrō, an example of Nara period bell tower Horyu-ji37s3200.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō, a typical ''hakamagoshi'' type Takidani-hudoumyououji-syourou.jpg|Takidani-hudōmyōō-ji Shōrō Todaiji shoro.jpg|Tōdai-ji's bell tower, an example of the ''fukihanachi'' type, although much larger than the average Komyoji01s3200.jpg|Sometimes the bell is installed in the ''[[rōmon]]''. 崇福寺 (岐阜市)-鐘楼soufukuji007.jpg| Taipei City Tone-Wa Tample Bell tower.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. 臨濟護國禪寺舊鐘樓山門.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]] ==References== {{commons Category|Bell towers in Japan}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Japanese architectural features]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist architecture]] [[Category:Bell towers]] [[Category:Shinto architecture]] }} [[en:Shōrō]] [[sn:Shōrō]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] {{Commons category|Bell towers in Japan}} [[de:Shōrō]] [[es:Shōrō]] [[eu:Shōrō]] [[fr:Shōrō]] [[id:Shōrō]] [[it:Shōrō]] [[ja:鐘楼]] [[wuu:钟楼（东亚）]] [[zh:鐘樓 (東亞)]]

Roofs are either {{nihongo|gabled|切妻造|[[:en:Gabled roof|kirizuma-zukuri]]}} or {{nihongo|hip-and-gable|入母屋造|[[:en:East Asian hip-and-gable roof|East Asian hip-and-gable roof]]}}. ==Gallery== Chion-in bell 1 by MShades in Kyoto.jpg|Great Bell at Chion-in Gousyouzi 11.jpg|Goshoji-Temple Horyuji-L0337.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Sai-in Shōrō, an example of Nara period bell tower Horyu-ji37s3200.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō, a typical ''hakamagoshi'' type Takidani-hudoumyououji-syourou.jpg|Takidani-hudōmyōō-ji Shōrō Todaiji shoro.jpg|Tōdai-ji's bell tower, an example of the ''fukihanachi'' type, although much larger than the average Komyoji01s3200.jpg|Sometimes the bell is installed in the ''[[rōmon]]''. 崇福寺 (岐阜市)-鐘楼soufukuji007.jpg| Taipei City Tone-Wa Tample Bell tower.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. 臨濟護國禪寺舊鐘樓山門.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]] ==References== {{commons Category|Bell towers in Japan}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Japanese architectural features]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist architecture]] [[Category:Bell towers]] [[Category:Shinto architecture]] }} [[en:Shōrō]] [[sn:Shōrō]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Bell towers in Japan}} [[de:Shōrō]] [[es:Shōrō]] [[eu:Shōrō]] [[fr:Shōrō]] [[id:Shōrō]] [[it:Shōrō]] [[ja:鐘楼]] [[wuu:钟楼（东亚）]] [[zh:鐘樓 (東亞)]]

{{Short description|Bell towers in Japan}} {{Italic title|reason=}}
{| style="border-collapse:collapse" cellpadding="0" |style="border:1px solid black;"|[[File:Keiunji Au10 08.JPG|alt=Wooden belltower, old style|x200px]] |style="border:1px solid black;"|[[File:Kencho-ji,-Bell.jpg|alt=Wooden belltower, new style|x200px]] |}
The two main types of bell tower in Japan
The {{nihongo|'''''shōrō''''', '''''shurō'''''|鐘楼|lit. bell building}} or {{nihongo|'''''kanetsuki-[[Dō (architecture)|dō]]'''''|鐘突堂|lit. bell-striking hall}} is the [[:en:Bell tower|Bell tower]] of a [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple in Japan]], housing the temple's {{nihongo|''[[:en:Bonshō|Bonshō]]''|梵鐘}}. It can also be found at some [[Shinto shrine]]s which used to function as temples (see article ''[[Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu shūgō]]''), as for example [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]. Two main types exist, the older {{nihongo|''hakamagoshi''|袴腰}}, which has walls, and the more recent {{nihongo|''fukihanachi''|吹放ち}} or {{nihongo|''fukinuki''|吹貫・吹抜き}}, which does not.{{cite book | title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi | editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku | publisher=Shōwa-dō | date=April 10, 1990 | edition=September 30, 2008 | isbn=4-8122-9805-9 | page=82|language=ja}} ==History== During the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] (710–794), immediately after the arrival of [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism in Japan]] bell towers were 3 x 2 [[:en:Ken (unit)|bay]], 2 storied buildings.{{cite web|title=Shourou|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shourou.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=22 September 2010}} A typical temple ''[[:en:Garan|Garan]]'' had normally two, one to the left and one to the right of the ''[[kyōzō]]'' (or ''kyō-dō''), the [[sūtra]] repository. An extant example of this style is [[Hōryū-ji]]'s Sai-in Shōrō in [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara]] (see photo in the gallery). During the following [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] (794–1185) was developed a new style called ''hakamagoshi'' which consisted of a two storied, [[:en:Hourglass|hourglass]]-shaped building with the bell hanging from the second story. The earliest extant example is Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō (see photo in the gallery). Finally, during the 13th century the ''fukihanachi'' type was created at [[Tōdai-ji]] by making all structural parts visible. The bell tower in this case usually consists of a 1-ken wide, 1-ken high structure with no walls and having the bell at its center (see photo above). Sometimes the four pillars have an inward inclination called {{nihongo|uchikorobi|内転び|lit. inward fall}}. After the Nara period, in which temple layout was rigidly prescribed after the Chinese fashion, the position of the bell tower stopped being prescribed and began to change temple by temple. Roofs are either {{nihongo|gabled|切妻造|[[:en:Gabled roof|kirizuma-zukuri]]}} or {{nihongo|hip-and-gable|入母屋造|[[:en:East Asian hip-and-gable roof|East Asian hip-and-gable roof]]}}. ==Gallery== Chion-in bell 1 by MShades in Kyoto.jpg|Great Bell at Chion-in Gousyouzi 11.jpg|Goshoji-Temple Horyuji-L0337.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Sai-in Shōrō, an example of Nara period bell tower Horyu-ji37s3200.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō, a typical ''hakamagoshi'' type Takidani-hudoumyououji-syourou.jpg|Takidani-hudōmyōō-ji Shōrō Todaiji shoro.jpg|Tōdai-ji's bell tower, an example of the ''fukihanachi'' type, although much larger than the average Komyoji01s3200.jpg|Sometimes the bell is installed in the ''[[rōmon]]''. 崇福寺 (岐阜市)-鐘楼soufukuji007.jpg| Taipei City Tone-Wa Tample Bell tower.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. 臨濟護國禪寺舊鐘樓山門.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]] ==References== {{commons Category|Bell towers in Japan}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Japanese architectural features]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist architecture]] [[Category:Bell towers]] [[Category:Shinto architecture]] }} [[en:Shōrō]] [[sn:Shōrō]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Bell towers in Japan}} [[de:Shōrō]] [[es:Shōrō]] [[eu:Shōrō]] [[fr:Shōrō]] [[id:Shōrō]] [[it:Shōrō]] [[ja:鐘楼]] [[wuu:钟楼（东亚）]] [[zh:鐘樓 (東亞)]]

Roofs are either {{nihongo|gabled|切妻造|[[:en:Gabled roof|kirizuma-zukuri]]}} or {{nihongo|hip-and-gable|入母屋造|[[:en:East Asian hip-and-gable roof|East Asian hip-and-gable roof]]}}. ==Gallery== Chion-in bell 1 by MShades in Kyoto.jpg|Great Bell at Chion-in Gousyouzi 11.jpg|Goshoji-Temple Horyuji-L0337.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Sai-in Shōrō, an example of Nara period bell tower Horyu-ji37s3200.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō, a typical ''hakamagoshi'' type Takidani-hudoumyououji-syourou.jpg|Takidani-hudōmyōō-ji Shōrō Todaiji shoro.jpg|Tōdai-ji's bell tower, an example of the ''fukihanachi'' type, although much larger than the average Komyoji01s3200.jpg|Sometimes the bell is installed in the ''[[:en:Rōmon|Rōmon]]''. 崇福寺 (岐阜市)-鐘楼soufukuji007.jpg| Taipei City Tone-Wa Tample Bell tower.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. 臨濟護國禪寺舊鐘樓山門.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]] ==References== {{commons Category|Bell towers in Japan}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Japanese architectural features]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist architecture]] [[Category:Bell towers]] [[Category:Shinto architecture]] }} [[en:Shōrō]] [[sn:Shōrō]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Bell towers in Japan}} [[de:Shōrō]] [[es:Shōrō]] [[eu:Shōrō]] [[fr:Shōrō]] [[id:Shōrō]] [[it:Shōrō]] [[ja:鐘楼]] [[wuu:钟楼（东亚）]] [[zh:鐘樓 (東亞)]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q1363585]] Wikidata name: shōrō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Bell towers in Japan}} {{Italic title|reason=}}
{| style="border-collapse:collapse" cellpadding="0" |style="border:1px solid black;"|[[File:Keiunji Au10 08.JPG|alt=Wooden belltower, old style|x200px]] |style="border:1px solid black;"|[[File:Kencho-ji,-Bell.jpg|alt=Wooden belltower, new style|x200px]] |}
The two main types of bell tower in Japan
The {{nihongo|'''''shōrō''''', '''''shurō'''''|鐘楼|lit. bell building}} or {{nihongo|'''''kanetsuki-[[Dō (architecture)|dō]]'''''|鐘突堂|lit. bell-striking hall}} is the [[:en:Bell tower|Bell tower]] of a [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple in Japan]], housing the temple's {{nihongo|''[[:en:Bonshō|Bonshō]]''|梵鐘}}. It can also be found at some [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s which used to function as temples (see article ''[[Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu shūgō]]''), as for example [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]. Two main types exist, the older {{nihongo|''hakamagoshi''|袴腰}}, which has walls, and the more recent {{nihongo|''fukihanachi''|吹放ち}} or {{nihongo|''fukinuki''|吹貫・吹抜き}}, which does not.{{cite book | title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi | editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku | publisher=Shōwa-dō | date=April 10, 1990 | edition=September 30, 2008 | isbn=4-8122-9805-9 | page=82|language=ja}} ==History== During the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] (710–794), immediately after the arrival of [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism in Japan]] bell towers were 3 x 2 [[:en:Ken (unit)|bay]], 2 storied buildings.{{cite web|title=Shourou|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shourou.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=22 September 2010}} A typical temple ''[[:en:Garan|Garan]]'' had normally two, one to the left and one to the right of the ''[[kyōzō]]'' (or ''kyō-dō''), the [[:en:Sutra|Sutra]] repository. An extant example of this style is [[Hōryū-ji]]'s Sai-in Shōrō in [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara]] (see photo in the gallery). During the following [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] (794–1185) was developed a new style called ''hakamagoshi'' which consisted of a two storied, [[:en:Hourglass|hourglass]]-shaped building with the bell hanging from the second story. The earliest extant example is Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō (see photo in the gallery). Finally, during the 13th century the ''fukihanachi'' type was created at [[Tōdai-ji]] by making all structural parts visible. The bell tower in this case usually consists of a 1-ken wide, 1-ken high structure with no walls and having the bell at its center (see photo above). Sometimes the four pillars have an inward inclination called {{nihongo|uchikorobi|内転び|lit. inward fall}}. After the Nara period, in which temple layout was rigidly prescribed after the Chinese fashion, the position of the bell tower stopped being prescribed and began to change temple by temple. Roofs are either {{nihongo|gabled|切妻造|[[:en:Gabled roof|kirizuma-zukuri]]}} or {{nihongo|hip-and-gable|入母屋造|[[:en:East Asian hip-and-gable roof|East Asian hip-and-gable roof]]}}. ==Gallery== Chion-in bell 1 by MShades in Kyoto.jpg|Great Bell at Chion-in Gousyouzi 11.jpg|Goshoji-Temple Horyuji-L0337.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Sai-in Shōrō, an example of Nara period bell tower Horyu-ji37s3200.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō, a typical ''hakamagoshi'' type Takidani-hudoumyououji-syourou.jpg|Takidani-hudōmyōō-ji Shōrō Todaiji shoro.jpg|Tōdai-ji's bell tower, an example of the ''fukihanachi'' type, although much larger than the average Komyoji01s3200.jpg|Sometimes the bell is installed in the ''[[:en:Rōmon|Rōmon]]''. 崇福寺 (岐阜市)-鐘楼soufukuji007.jpg| Taipei City Tone-Wa Tample Bell tower.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. 臨濟護國禪寺舊鐘樓山門.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]] ==References== {{commons Category|Bell towers in Japan}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Japanese architectural features]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist architecture]] [[Category:Bell towers]] [[Category:Shinto architecture]] }} [[en:Shōrō]] [[sn:Shōrō]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Bell towers in Japan}} [[de:Shōrō]] [[es:Shōrō]] [[eu:Shōrō]] [[fr:Shōrō]] [[id:Shōrō]] [[it:Shōrō]] [[ja:鐘楼]] [[wuu:钟楼（东亚）]] [[zh:鐘樓 (東亞)]]

During the following [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] (794–1185) was developed a new style called ''hakamagoshi'' which consisted of a two storied, [[:en:Hourglass|hourglass]]-shaped building with the bell hanging from the second story. The earliest extant example is Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō (see photo in the gallery). Finally, during the 13th century the ''fukihanachi'' type was created at [[Tōdai-ji]] by making all structural parts visible. The bell tower in this case usually consists of a 1-ken wide, 1-ken high structure with no walls and having the bell at its center (see photo above). Sometimes the four pillars have an inward inclination called {{nihongo|uchikorobi|内転び|lit. inward fall}}. After the Nara period, in which temple layout was rigidly prescribed after the Chinese fashion, the position of the bell tower stopped being prescribed and began to change temple by temple. Roofs are either {{nihongo|gabled|切妻造|[[:en:Gabled roof|kirizuma-zukuri]]}} or {{nihongo|hip-and-gable|入母屋造|[[:en:East Asian hip-and-gable roof|East Asian hip-and-gable roof]]}}. ==Gallery== Chion-in bell 1 by MShades in Kyoto.jpg|Great Bell at Chion-in Gousyouzi 11.jpg|Goshoji-Temple Horyuji-L0337.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Sai-in Shōrō, an example of Nara period bell tower Horyu-ji37s3200.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō, a typical ''hakamagoshi'' type Takidani-hudoumyououji-syourou.jpg|Takidani-hudōmyōō-ji Shōrō Todaiji shoro.jpg|Tōdai-ji's bell tower, an example of the ''fukihanachi'' type, although much larger than the average Komyoji01s3200.jpg|Sometimes the bell is installed in the ''[[:en:Rōmon|Rōmon]]''. 崇福寺 (岐阜市)-鐘楼soufukuji007.jpg| Taipei City Tone-Wa Tample Bell tower.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. 臨濟護國禪寺舊鐘樓山門.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]] ==References== {{commons Category|Bell towers in Japan}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Japanese architectural features]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist architecture]] [[Category:Bell towers]] [[Category:Shinto architecture]] }} [[en:Shōrō]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Bell towers in Japan}} [[de:Shōrō]] [[es:Shōrō]] [[eu:Shōrō]] [[fr:Shōrō]] [[id:Shōrō]] [[it:Shōrō]] [[ja:鐘楼]] [[wuu:钟楼（东亚）]] [[zh:鐘樓 (東亞)]] {{wikidata link|Q1363585}}

Roofs are either {{nihongo|gabled|切妻造|[[:en:Gabled roof|kirizuma-zukuri]]}} or {{nihongo|hip-and-gable|入母屋造|[[:en:East Asian hip-and-gable roof|East Asian hip-and-gable roof]]}}. ==Gallery== Chion-in bell 1 by MShades in Kyoto.jpg|Great Bell at Chion-in Gousyouzi 11.jpg|Goshoji-Temple Horyuji-L0337.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Sai-in Shōrō, an example of Nara period bell tower Horyu-ji37s3200.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō, a typical ''hakamagoshi'' type Takidani-hudoumyououji-syourou.jpg|Takidani-hudōmyōō-ji Shōrō Todaiji shoro.jpg|Tōdai-ji's bell tower, an example of the ''fukihanachi'' type, although much larger than the average Komyoji01s3200.jpg|Sometimes the bell is installed in the ''[[:en:Rōmon|Rōmon]]''. 崇福寺 (岐阜市)-鐘楼soufukuji007.jpg| Taipei City Tone-Wa Tample Bell tower.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. 臨濟護國禪寺舊鐘樓山門.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]] ==References== {{commons Category|Bell towers in Japan}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Japanese architectural features]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist architecture]] [[Category:Bell towers]] [[Category:Shinto architecture]] }} [[en:Shōrō]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Bell towers in Japan}} [[de:Shōrō]] [[es:Shōrō]] [[eu:Shōrō]] [[fr:Shōrō]] [[id:Shōrō]] [[it:Shōrō]] [[ja:鐘楼]] [[wuu:钟楼（东亚）]] [[zh:鐘樓 (東亞)]] {{wikidata link|Q1363585}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

{| style="border-collapse:collapse" cellpadding="0" |style="border:1px solid black;"|[[File:Keiunji Au10 08.JPG|alt=Wooden belltower, old style|x200px]] |style="border:1px solid black;"|[[File:Kencho-ji,-Bell.jpg|alt=Wooden belltower, new style|x200px]] |}
The two main types of bell tower in Japan
The {{nihongo|'''''shōrō''''', '''''shurō'''''|鐘楼|lit. bell building}} or {{nihongo|'''''kanetsuki-[[Dō (architecture)|dō]]'''''|鐘突堂|lit. bell-striking hall}} is the [[:en:Bell tower|Bell tower]] of a [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple in Japan]], housing the temple's {{nihongo|''[[:en:Bonshō|Bonshō]]''|梵鐘}}. It can also be found at some [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s which used to function as temples (see article ''[[Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu shūgō]]''), as for example [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]. Two main types exist, the older {{nihongo|''hakamagoshi''|袴腰}}, which has walls, and the more recent {{nihongo|''fukihanachi''|吹放ち}} or {{nihongo|''fukinuki''|吹貫・吹抜き}}, which does not.{{cite book | title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi | editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku | publisher=Shōwa-dō | date=April 10, 1990 | edition=September 30, 2008 | isbn=4-8122-9805-9 | page=82|language=ja}} ==History== During the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] (710–794), immediately after the arrival of [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism in Japan]] bell towers were 3 x 2 [[:en:Ken (unit)|bay]], 2 storied buildings.{{cite web|title=Shourou|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shourou.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=22 September 2010}} A typical temple ''[[:en:Garan|Garan]]'' had normally two, one to the left and one to the right of the ''[[kyōzō]]'' (or ''kyō-dō''), the [[:en:Sutra|Sutra]] repository. An extant example of this style is [[Hōryū-ji]]'s Sai-in Shōrō in [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara]] (see photo in the gallery). During the following [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] (794–1185) was developed a new style called ''hakamagoshi'' which consisted of a two storied, [[:en:Hourglass|hourglass]]-shaped building with the bell hanging from the second story. The earliest extant example is Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō (see photo in the gallery). Finally, during the 13th century the ''fukihanachi'' type was created at [[Tōdai-ji]] by making all structural parts visible. The bell tower in this case usually consists of a 1-ken wide, 1-ken high structure with no walls and having the bell at its center (see photo above). Sometimes the four pillars have an inward inclination called {{nihongo|uchikorobi|内転び|lit. inward fall}}. After the Nara period, in which temple layout was rigidly prescribed after the Chinese fashion, the position of the bell tower stopped being prescribed and began to change temple by temple. Roofs are either {{nihongo|gabled|切妻造|[[:en:Gabled roof|kirizuma-zukuri]]}} or {{nihongo|hip-and-gable|入母屋造|[[:en:East Asian hip-and-gable roof|East Asian hip-and-gable roof]]}}. ==Gallery== Chion-in bell 1 by MShades in Kyoto.jpg|Great Bell at Chion-in Gousyouzi 11.jpg|Goshoji-Temple Horyuji-L0337.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Sai-in Shōrō, an example of Nara period bell tower Horyu-ji37s3200.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō, a typical ''hakamagoshi'' type Takidani-hudoumyououji-syourou.jpg|Takidani-hudōmyōō-ji Shōrō Todaiji shoro.jpg|Tōdai-ji's bell tower, an example of the ''fukihanachi'' type, although much larger than the average Komyoji01s3200.jpg|Sometimes the bell is installed in the ''[[:en:Rōmon|Rōmon]]''. 崇福寺 (岐阜市)-鐘楼soufukuji007.jpg| Taipei City Tone-Wa Tample Bell tower.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. 臨濟護國禪寺舊鐘樓山門.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]] ==References== {{commons Category|Bell towers in Japan}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Japanese architectural features]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist architecture]] [[Category:Bell towers]] [[Category:Shinto architecture]] }} [[en:Shōrō]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Bell towers in Japan}} [[de:Shōrō]] [[es:Shōrō]] [[eu:Shōrō]] [[fr:Shōrō]] [[id:Shōrō]] [[it:Shōrō]] [[ja:鐘楼]] [[wuu:钟楼（东亚）]] [[zh:鐘樓 (東亞)]] {{wikidata link|Q1363585}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q1363585}} [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

{| style="border-collapse:collapse" cellpadding="0" |style="border:1px solid black;"|[[File:Keiunji Au10 08.JPG|alt=Wooden belltower, old style|x200px]] |style="border:1px solid black;"|[[File:Kencho-ji,-Bell.jpg|alt=Wooden belltower, new style|x200px]] |}
The two main types of bell tower in Japan
The {{nihongo|'''''shōrō''''', '''''shurō'''''|鐘楼|lit. bell building}} or {{nihongo|'''''kanetsuki-[[Dō (architecture)|dō]]'''''|鐘突堂|lit. bell-striking hall}} is the [[:en:Bell tower|Bell tower]] of a [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple in Japan]], housing the temple's {{nihongo|''[[:en:Bonshō|Bonshō]]''|梵鐘}}. It can also be found at some [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s which used to function as temples (see article ''[[Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu shūgō]]''), as for example [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]. Two main types exist, the older {{nihongo|''hakamagoshi''|袴腰}}, which has walls, and the more recent {{nihongo|''fukihanachi''|吹放ち}} or {{nihongo|''fukinuki''|吹貫・吹抜き}}, which does not.{{cite book | title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi | editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku | publisher=Shōwa-dō | date=April 10, 1990 | edition=September 30, 2008 | isbn=4-8122-9805-9 | page=82|language=ja}} ==History== During the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] (710–794), immediately after the arrival of [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism in Japan]] bell towers were 3 x 2 [[:en:Ken (unit)|bay]], 2 storied buildings.{{cite web|title=Shourou|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shourou.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=22 September 2010}} A typical temple ''[[:en:Garan|Garan]]'' had normally two, one to the left and one to the right of the ''[[kyōzō]]'' (or ''kyō-dō''), the [[:en:Sutra|Sutra]] repository. An extant example of this style is [[Hōryū-ji]]'s Sai-in Shōrō in [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara]] (see photo in the gallery). During the following [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] (794–1185) was developed a new style called ''hakamagoshi'' which consisted of a two storied, [[:en:Hourglass|hourglass]]-shaped building with the bell hanging from the second story. The earliest extant example is Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō (see photo in the gallery). Finally, during the 13th century the ''fukihanachi'' type was created at [[Tōdai-ji]] by making all structural parts visible. The bell tower in this case usually consists of a 1-ken wide, 1-ken high structure with no walls and having the bell at its center (see photo above). Sometimes the four pillars have an inward inclination called {{nihongo|uchikorobi|内転び|lit. inward fall}}. After the Nara period, in which temple layout was rigidly prescribed after the Chinese fashion, the position of the bell tower stopped being prescribed and began to change temple by temple. Roofs are either {{nihongo|gabled|切妻造|[[:en:Gabled roof|kirizuma-zukuri]]}} or {{nihongo|hip-and-gable|入母屋造|[[:en:East Asian hip-and-gable roof|East Asian hip-and-gable roof]]}}. ==Gallery== Chion-in bell 1 by MShades in Kyoto.jpg|Great Bell at Chion-in Gousyouzi 11.jpg|Goshoji-Temple Horyuji-L0337.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Sai-in Shōrō, an example of Nara period bell tower Horyu-ji37s3200.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō, a typical ''hakamagoshi'' type Takidani-hudoumyououji-syourou.jpg|Takidani-hudōmyōō-ji Shōrō Todaiji shoro.jpg|Tōdai-ji's bell tower, an example of the ''fukihanachi'' type, although much larger than the average Komyoji01s3200.jpg|Sometimes the bell is installed in the ''[[:en:Rōmon|Rōmon]]''. 崇福寺 (岐阜市)-鐘楼soufukuji007.jpg| Taipei City Tone-Wa Tample Bell tower.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. 臨濟護國禪寺舊鐘樓山門.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]] ==References== {{commons Category|Bell towers in Japan}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Japanese architectural features]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist architecture]] [[Category:Bell towers]] [[Category:Shinto architecture]] }} [[en:Shōrō]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Bell towers in Japan}} [[de:Shōrō]] [[es:Shōrō]] [[eu:Shōrō]] [[fr:Shōrō]] [[id:Shōrō]] [[it:Shōrō]] [[ja:鐘楼]] [[wuu:钟楼（东亚）]] [[zh:鐘樓 (東亞)]] {{wikidata link|Q1363585}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q1363585}} [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

[[commons:Category:Bell towers in Japan]][[de:Shōrō]][[en:Shōrō]][[es:Shōrō]][[eu:Shōrō]][[fr:Shōrō]][[id:Shōrō]][[it:Shōrō]][[ja:鐘楼]][[wuu:钟楼（东亚）]][[zh:鐘樓 (東亞)]] [[commons:Category:Bell towers in Japan]][[de:Shōrō]][[en:Shōrō]][[es:Shōrō]][[eu:Shōrō]][[fr:Shōrō]][[id:Shōrō]][[it:Shōrō]][[ja:鐘楼]][[wuu:钟楼（东亚）]][[zh:鐘樓 (東亞)]] [[sn:commons:Category:Bell towers in Japan]][[de:Shōrō]][[en:Shōrō]][[es:Shōrō]][[eu:Shōrō]][[fr:Shōrō]][[id:Shōrō]][[it:Shōrō]][[ja:鐘楼]][[wuu:钟楼（东亚）]][[zh:鐘樓 (東亞)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1363585]] Wikidata name: shōrō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Bell towers in Japan}} {{Italic title|reason=}}
{| style="border-collapse:collapse" cellpadding="0" |style="border:1px solid black;"|[[File:Keiunji Au10 08.JPG|alt=Wooden belltower, old style|x200px]] |style="border:1px solid black;"|[[File:Kencho-ji,-Bell.jpg|alt=Wooden belltower, new style|x200px]] |}
The two main types of bell tower in Japan
The {{nihongo|'''''shōrō''''', '''''shurō'''''|鐘楼|lit. bell building}} or {{nihongo|'''''kanetsuki-[[Dō (architecture)|dō]]'''''|鐘突堂|lit. bell-striking hall}} is the [[:en:Bell tower|Bell tower]] of a [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple in Japan]], housing the temple's {{nihongo|''[[:en:Bonshō|Bonshō]]''|梵鐘}}. It can also be found at some [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s which used to function as temples (see article ''[[Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu shūgō]]''), as for example [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]. Two main types exist, the older {{nihongo|''hakamagoshi''|袴腰}}, which has walls, and the more recent {{nihongo|''fukihanachi''|吹放ち}} or {{nihongo|''fukinuki''|吹貫・吹抜き}}, which does not.{{cite book | title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi | editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku | publisher=Shōwa-dō | date=April 10, 1990 | edition=September 30, 2008 | isbn=4-8122-9805-9 | page=82|language=ja}} ==History== During the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] (710–794), immediately after the arrival of [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism in Japan]] bell towers were 3 x 2 [[:en:Ken (unit)|bay]], 2 storied buildings.{{cite web|title=Shourou|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shourou.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=22 September 2010}} A typical temple ''[[:en:Garan|Garan]]'' had normally two, one to the left and one to the right of the ''[[kyōzō]]'' (or ''kyō-dō''), the [[:en:Sutra|Sutra]] repository. An extant example of this style is [[Hōryū-ji]]'s Sai-in Shōrō in [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara]] (see photo in the gallery). During the following [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] (794–1185) was developed a new style called ''hakamagoshi'' which consisted of a two storied, [[:en:Hourglass|hourglass]]-shaped building with the bell hanging from the second story. The earliest extant example is Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō (see photo in the gallery). Finally, during the 13th century the ''fukihanachi'' type was created at [[Tōdai-ji]] by making all structural parts visible. The bell tower in this case usually consists of a 1-ken wide, 1-ken high structure with no walls and having the bell at its center (see photo above). Sometimes the four pillars have an inward inclination called {{nihongo|uchikorobi|内転び|lit. inward fall}}. After the Nara period, in which temple layout was rigidly prescribed after the Chinese fashion, the position of the bell tower stopped being prescribed and began to change temple by temple. Roofs are either {{nihongo|gabled|切妻造|[[:en:Gabled roof|kirizuma-zukuri]]}} or {{nihongo|hip-and-gable|入母屋造|[[:en:East Asian hip-and-gable roof|East Asian hip-and-gable roof]]}}. ==Gallery== Chion-in bell 1 by MShades in Kyoto.jpg|Great Bell at Chion-in Gousyouzi 11.jpg|Goshoji-Temple Horyuji-L0337.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Sai-in Shōrō, an example of Nara period bell tower Horyu-ji37s3200.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō, a typical ''hakamagoshi'' type Takidani-hudoumyououji-syourou.jpg|Takidani-hudōmyōō-ji Shōrō Todaiji shoro.jpg|Tōdai-ji's bell tower, an example of the ''fukihanachi'' type, although much larger than the average Komyoji01s3200.jpg|Sometimes the bell is installed in the ''[[:en:Rōmon|Rōmon]]''. 崇福寺 (岐阜市)-鐘楼soufukuji007.jpg| Taipei City Tone-Wa Tample Bell tower.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. 臨濟護國禪寺舊鐘樓山門.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]] ==References== {{commons Category|Bell towers in Japan}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Japanese architectural features]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist architecture]] [[Category:Bell towers]] [[Category:Shinto architecture]] }} [[en:Shōrō]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Bell towers in Japan}} [[de:Shōrō]] [[es:Shōrō]] [[eu:Shōrō]] [[fr:Shōrō]] [[id:Shōrō]] [[it:Shōrō]] [[ja:鐘楼]] [[wuu:钟楼（东亚）]] [[zh:鐘樓 (東亞)]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q1363585}}

Roofs are either {{nihongo|gabled|切妻造|[[:en:Gabled roof|kirizuma-zukuri]]}} or {{nihongo|hip-and-gable|入母屋造|[[:en:East Asian hip-and-gable roof|East Asian hip-and-gable roof]]}}. ==Gallery== Chion-in bell 1 by MShades in Kyoto.jpg|Great Bell at Chion-in Gousyouzi 11.jpg|Goshoji-Temple Horyuji-L0337.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Sai-in Shōrō, an example of Nara period bell tower Horyu-ji37s3200.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō, a typical ''hakamagoshi'' type Takidani-hudoumyououji-syourou.jpg|Takidani-hudōmyōō-ji Shōrō Todaiji shoro.jpg|Tōdai-ji's bell tower, an example of the ''fukihanachi'' type, although much larger than the average Komyoji01s3200.jpg|Sometimes the bell is installed in the ''[[:en:Rōmon|Rōmon]]''. 崇福寺 (岐阜市)-鐘楼soufukuji007.jpg| Taipei City Tone-Wa Tample Bell tower.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. 臨濟護國禪寺舊鐘樓山門.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]] ==References== {{commons Category|Bell towers in Japan}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{draft categories| }} [[en:Shōrō]] {{Commons category|Bell towers in Japan}} [[de:Shōrō]] [[es:Shōrō]] [[eu:Shōrō]] [[fr:Shōrō]] [[id:Shōrō]] [[it:Shōrō]] [[ja:鐘楼]] [[wuu:钟楼（东亚）]] [[zh:鐘樓 (東亞)]] {{wikidata link|Q1363585}} [[Category:Bell towers]] [[Category:Japanese architectural features]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist architecture]] [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shinto architecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]]

{| style="border-collapse:collapse" cellpadding="0" |style="border:1px solid black;"|[[File:Keiunji Au10 08.JPG|alt=Wooden belltower, old style|x200px]] |style="border:1px solid black;"|[[File:Kencho-ji,-Bell.jpg|alt=Wooden belltower, new style|x200px]] |}
The two main types of bell tower in Japan
The {{nihongo|'''''shōrō''''', '''''shurō'''''|鐘楼|lit. bell building}} or {{nihongo|'''''kanetsuki-[[Dō (architecture)|dō]]'''''|鐘突堂|lit. bell-striking hall}} is the [[:en:Bell tower|Bell tower]] of a [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple in Japan]], housing the temple's {{nihongo|''[[:en:Bonshō|Bonshō]]''|梵鐘}}. It can also be found at some [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s which used to function as temples (see article ''[[Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu shūgō]]''), as for example [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]. Two main types exist, the older {{nihongo|''hakamagoshi''|袴腰}}, which has walls, and the more recent {{nihongo|''fukihanachi''|吹放ち}} or {{nihongo|''fukinuki''|吹貫・吹抜き}}, which does not.{{cite book | title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi | editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku | publisher=Shōwa-dō | date=April 10, 1990 | edition=September 30, 2008 | isbn=4-8122-9805-9 | page=82|language=ja}} ==History== During the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] (710–794), immediately after the arrival of [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism in Japan]] bell towers were 3 x 2 [[:en:Ken (unit)|bay]], 2 storied buildings.{{cite web|title=Shourou|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shourou.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=22 September 2010}} A typical temple ''[[:en:Garan|Garan]]'' had normally two, one to the left and one to the right of the ''[[kyōzō]]'' (or ''kyō-dō''), the [[:en:Sutra|Sutra]] repository. An extant example of this style is [[Hōryū-ji]]'s Sai-in Shōrō in [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara]] (see photo in the gallery). During the following [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] (794–1185) was developed a new style called ''hakamagoshi'' which consisted of a two storied, [[:en:Hourglass|hourglass]]-shaped building with the bell hanging from the second story. The earliest extant example is Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō (see photo in the gallery). Finally, during the 13th century the ''fukihanachi'' type was created at [[Tōdai-ji]] by making all structural parts visible. The bell tower in this case usually consists of a 1-ken wide, 1-ken high structure with no walls and having the bell at its center (see photo above). Sometimes the four pillars have an inward inclination called {{nihongo|uchikorobi|内転び|lit. inward fall}}. After the Nara period, in which temple layout was rigidly prescribed after the Chinese fashion, the position of the bell tower stopped being prescribed and began to change temple by temple. Roofs are either {{nihongo|gabled|切妻造|[[:en:Gabled roof|kirizuma-zukuri]]}} or {{nihongo|hip-and-gable|入母屋造|[[:en:East Asian hip-and-gable roof|East Asian hip-and-gable roof]]}}. ==Gallery== Chion-in bell 1 by MShades in Kyoto.jpg|Great Bell at Chion-in Gousyouzi 11.jpg|Goshoji-Temple Horyuji-L0337.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Sai-in Shōrō, an example of Nara period bell tower Horyu-ji37s3200.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō, a typical ''hakamagoshi'' type Takidani-hudoumyououji-syourou.jpg|Takidani-hudōmyōō-ji Shōrō Todaiji shoro.jpg|Tōdai-ji's bell tower, an example of the ''fukihanachi'' type, although much larger than the average Komyoji01s3200.jpg|Sometimes the bell is installed in the ''[[:en:Rōmon|Rōmon]]''. 崇福寺 (岐阜市)-鐘楼soufukuji007.jpg| Taipei City Tone-Wa Tample Bell tower.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. 臨濟護國禪寺舊鐘樓山門.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]] ==References== {{commons Category|Bell towers in Japan}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{draft categories| }} [[en:Shōrō]] {{Commons category|Bell towers in Japan}} [[de:Shōrō]] [[es:Shōrō]] [[eu:Shōrō]] [[fr:Shōrō]] [[id:Shōrō]] [[it:Shōrō]] [[ja:鐘楼]] [[wuu:钟楼（东亚）]] [[zh:鐘樓 (東亞)]] {{wikidata link|Q1363585}} [[Category:Bell towers]] [[Category:Japanese architectural features]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist architecture]] [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shinto architecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]]
