Roofs are either {{nihongo|gabled|切妻造|[[:en:Gabled roof|kirizuma-zukuri]]}} or {{nihongo|hip-and-gable|入母屋造|[[:en:East Asian hip-and-gable roof|East Asian hip-and-gable roof]]}}. ==Gallery== Chion-in bell 1 by MShades in Kyoto.jpg|Great Bell at Chion-in Gousyouzi 11.jpg|Goshoji-Temple Horyuji-L0337.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Sai-in Shōrō, an example of Nara period bell tower Horyu-ji37s3200.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō, a typical ''hakamagoshi'' type Takidani-hudoumyououji-syourou.jpg|Takidani-hudōmyōō-ji Shōrō Todaiji shoro.jpg|Tōdai-ji's bell tower, an example of the ''fukihanachi'' type, although much larger than the average Komyoji01s3200.jpg|Sometimes the bell is installed in the ''[[:en:Rōmon|Rōmon]]''. 崇福寺 (岐阜市)-鐘楼soufukuji007.jpg| Taipei City Tone-Wa Tample Bell tower.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. 臨濟護國禪寺舊鐘樓山門.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]] ==References== {{commons Category|Bell towers in Japan}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} [[en:Shōrō]] {{Commons category|Bell towers in Japan}} [[de:Shōrō]] [[es:Shōrō]] [[eu:Shōrō]] [[fr:Shōrō]] [[id:Shōrō]] [[it:Shōrō]] [[ja:鐘楼]] [[wuu:钟楼（东亚）]] [[zh:鐘樓 (東亞)]] {{wikidata link|Q1363585}} [[Category:Bell towers]] [[Category:Japanese architectural features]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist architecture]] [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shinto architecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]]

[[co:commons:Category:Bell towers in Japan]][[de:Shōrō]][[en:Shōrō]][[es:Shōrō]][[eu:Shōrō]][[fr:Shōrō]][[id:Shōrō]][[it:Shōrō]][[ja:鐘楼]][[wuu:钟楼（东亚）]][[zh:鐘樓 (東亞)]] [[co:commons:Category:Bell towers in Japan]][[de:Shōrō]][[en:Shōrō]][[es:Shōrō]][[eu:Shōrō]][[fr:Shōrō]][[id:Shōrō]][[it:Shōrō]][[ja:鐘楼]][[wuu:钟楼（东亚）]][[zh:鐘樓 (東亞)]] [[sn:commons:Category:Bell towers in Japan]][[de:Shōrō]][[en:Shōrō]][[es:Shōrō]][[eu:Shōrō]][[fr:Shōrō]][[id:Shōrō]][[it:Shōrō]][[ja:鐘楼]][[wuu:钟楼（东亚）]][[zh:鐘樓 (東亞)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1363585]] Wikidata name: shōrō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Bell towers in Japan}} {{Italic title|reason=}}
{| style="border-collapse:collapse" cellpadding="0" |style="border:1px solid black;"|[[File:Keiunji Au10 08.JPG|alt=Wooden belltower, old style|x200px]] |style="border:1px solid black;"|[[File:Kencho-ji,-Bell.jpg|alt=Wooden belltower, new style|x200px]] |}
The two main types of bell tower in Japan
The {{nihongo|'''''shōrō''''', '''''shurō'''''|鐘楼|lit. bell building}} or {{nihongo|'''''kanetsuki-[[Dō (architecture)|dō]]'''''|鐘突堂|lit. bell-striking hall}} is the [[:en:Bell tower|Bell tower]] of a [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple in Japan]], housing the temple's {{nihongo|''[[:en:Bonshō|Bonshō]]''|梵鐘}}. It can also be found at some [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s which used to function as temples (see article ''[[Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu shūgō]]''), as for example [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]. Two main types exist, the older {{nihongo|''hakamagoshi''|袴腰}}, which has walls, and the more recent {{nihongo|''fukihanachi''|吹放ち}} or {{nihongo|''fukinuki''|吹貫・吹抜き}}, which does not.{{cite book | title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi | editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku | publisher=Shōwa-dō | date=April 10, 1990 | edition=September 30, 2008 | isbn=4-8122-9805-9 | page=82|language=ja}} ==History== During the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] (710–794), immediately after the arrival of [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism in Japan]] bell towers were 3 x 2 [[:en:Ken (unit)|bay]], 2 storied buildings.{{cite web|title=Shourou|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shourou.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=22 September 2010}} A typical temple ''[[:en:Garan|Garan]]'' had normally two, one to the left and one to the right of the ''[[kyōzō]]'' (or ''kyō-dō''), the [[:en:Sutra|Sutra]] repository. An extant example of this style is [[Hōryū-ji]]'s Sai-in Shōrō in [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara]] (see photo in the gallery). During the following [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] (794–1185) was developed a new style called ''hakamagoshi'' which consisted of a two storied, [[:en:Hourglass|hourglass]]-shaped building with the bell hanging from the second story. The earliest extant example is Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō (see photo in the gallery). Finally, during the 13th century the ''fukihanachi'' type was created at [[Tōdai-ji]] by making all structural parts visible. The bell tower in this case usually consists of a 1-ken wide, 1-ken high structure with no walls and having the bell at its center (see photo above). Sometimes the four pillars have an inward inclination called {{nihongo|uchikorobi|内転び|lit. inward fall}}. After the Nara period, in which temple layout was rigidly prescribed after the Chinese fashion, the position of the bell tower stopped being prescribed and began to change temple by temple. Roofs are either {{nihongo|gabled|切妻造|[[:en:Gabled roof|kirizuma-zukuri]]}} or {{nihongo|hip-and-gable|入母屋造|[[:en:East Asian hip-and-gable roof|East Asian hip-and-gable roof]]}}. ==Gallery== Chion-in bell 1 by MShades in Kyoto.jpg|Great Bell at Chion-in Gousyouzi 11.jpg|Goshoji-Temple Horyuji-L0337.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Sai-in Shōrō, an example of Nara period bell tower Horyu-ji37s3200.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō, a typical ''hakamagoshi'' type Takidani-hudoumyououji-syourou.jpg|Takidani-hudōmyōō-ji Shōrō Todaiji shoro.jpg|Tōdai-ji's bell tower, an example of the ''fukihanachi'' type, although much larger than the average Komyoji01s3200.jpg|Sometimes the bell is installed in the ''[[:en:Rōmon|Rōmon]]''. 崇福寺 (岐阜市)-鐘楼soufukuji007.jpg| Taipei City Tone-Wa Tample Bell tower.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. 臨濟護國禪寺舊鐘樓山門.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]] ==References== {{commons Category|Bell towers in Japan}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} [[en:Shōrō]] {{Commons category|Bell towers in Japan}} [[de:Shōrō]] [[es:Shōrō]] [[eu:Shōrō]] [[fr:Shōrō]] [[id:Shōrō]] [[it:Shōrō]] [[ja:鐘楼]] [[wuu:钟楼（东亚）]] [[zh:鐘樓 (東亞)]] {{wikidata link|Q1363585}} [[Category:Bell towers]] [[Category:Japanese architectural features]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist architecture]] [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shinto architecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:qq]]

The {{nihongo|'''''shōrō''''', '''''shurō'''''|鐘楼|lit. bell building}} or {{nihongo|'''''kanetsuki-[[Dō (architecture)|dō]]'''''|鐘突堂|lit. bell-striking hall}} is the {{ill|Bell tower|en|Bell tower|WD=Q200334|lt=Bell tower}} of a {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|WD=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temple in Japan}}, housing the temple's {{nihongo|''{{ill|Bonshō|en|Bonshō|WD=Q8192099|lt=Bonshō}}''|梵鐘}}. It can also be found at some {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}}s which used to function as temples (see article ''[[Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu shūgō]]''), as for example {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|WD=Q696641|lt=Nikkō Tōshō-gū}}. An extant example of this style is [[Hōryū-ji]]'s Sai-in Shōrō in {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara}} (see photo in the gallery). During the following {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}} (794–1185) was developed a new style called ''hakamagoshi'' which consisted of a two storied, {{ill|Hourglass|en|Hourglass|WD=Q179904|lt=hourglass}}-shaped building with the bell hanging from the second story. The earliest extant example is Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō (see photo in the gallery). Finally, during the 13th century the ''fukihanachi'' type was created at [[Tōdai-ji]] by making all structural parts visible. The bell tower in this case usually consists of a 1-ken wide, 1-ken high structure with no walls and having the bell at its center (see photo above). Sometimes the four pillars have an inward inclination called {{nihongo|uchikorobi|内転び|lit. inward fall}}. After the Nara period, in which temple layout was rigidly prescribed after the Chinese fashion, the position of the bell tower stopped being prescribed and began to change temple by temple. Roofs are either {{nihongo|gabled|切妻造|[[:en:Gabled roof|kirizuma-zukuri]]}} or {{nihongo|hip-and-gable|入母屋造|{{ill|East Asian hip-and-gable roof|en|East Asian hip-and-gable roof|WD=Q855025|lt=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}}}}. ==Gallery== Chion-in bell 1 by MShades in Kyoto.jpg|Great Bell at Chion-in Gousyouzi 11.jpg|Goshoji-Temple Horyuji-L0337.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Sai-in Shōrō, an example of Nara period bell tower Horyu-ji37s3200.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō, a typical ''hakamagoshi'' type Takidani-hudoumyououji-syourou.jpg|Takidani-hudōmyōō-ji Shōrō Todaiji shoro.jpg|Tōdai-ji's bell tower, an example of the ''fukihanachi'' type, although much larger than the average Komyoji01s3200.jpg|Sometimes the bell is installed in the ''{{ill|Rōmon|en|Rōmon|WD=Q3458957|lt=Rōmon}}''. 崇福寺 (岐阜市)-鐘楼soufukuji007.jpg| Taipei City Tone-Wa Tample Bell tower.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. 臨濟護國禪寺舊鐘樓山門.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]] ==References== {{commons Category|Bell towers in Japan}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} [[en:Shōrō]] {{Commons category|Bell towers in Japan}} [[de:Shōrō]] [[es:Shōrō]] [[eu:Shōrō]] [[fr:Shōrō]] [[id:Shōrō]] [[it:Shōrō]] [[ja:鐘楼]] [[wuu:钟楼（东亚）]] [[zh:鐘樓 (東亞)]] {{wikidata link|Q1363585}} [[Category:Bell towers]] [[Category:Japanese architectural features]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist architecture]] [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shinto architecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

The {{nihongo|'''''shōrō''''', '''''shurō'''''|鐘楼|lit. bell building}} or {{nihongo|'''''kanetsuki-[[Dō (architecture)|dō]]'''''|鐘突堂|lit. bell-striking hall}} is the {{ill|Bell tower|en|Bell tower|WD=Q200334|lt=Bell tower}} of a {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|WD=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temple in Japan}}, housing the temple's {{nihongo|''{{ill|Bonshō|en|Bonshō|WD=Q8192099|lt=Bonshō}}''|梵鐘}}. It can also be found at some {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}}s which used to function as temples (see article ''[[Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu shūgō]]''), as for example {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|WD=Q696641|lt=Nikkō Tōshō-gū}}. Two main types exist, the older {{nihongo|''hakamagoshi''|袴腰}}, which has walls, and the more recent {{nihongo|''fukihanachi''|吹放ち}} or {{nihongo|''fukinuki''|吹貫・吹抜き}}, which does not.{{cite book | title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi | editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku | publisher=Shōwa-dō | date=April 10, 1990 | edition=September 30, 2008 | isbn=4-8122-9805-9 | page=82|language=ja}} ==History== During the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}} (710–794), immediately after the arrival of {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|WD=Q736311|lt=Buddhism in Japan}} bell towers were 3 x 2 {{ill|Ken (unit)|en|Ken (unit)|WD=Q3195007|lt=bay}}, 2 storied buildings.{{cite web|title=Shourou|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shourou.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=22 September 2010}} A typical temple ''[[:en:Garan|Garan]]'' had normally two, one to the left and one to the right of the ''[[kyōzō]]'' (or ''kyō-dō''), the {{ill|Sutra|en|Sutra|WD=Q209680|lt=Sutra}} repository. An extant example of this style is [[Hōryū-ji]]'s Sai-in Shōrō in {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara}} (see photo in the gallery). During the following {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}} (794–1185) was developed a new style called ''hakamagoshi'' which consisted of a two storied, {{ill|Hourglass|en|Hourglass|WD=Q179904|lt=hourglass}}-shaped building with the bell hanging from the second story. The earliest extant example is Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō (see photo in the gallery). Finally, during the 13th century the ''fukihanachi'' type was created at [[Tōdai-ji]] by making all structural parts visible. The bell tower in this case usually consists of a 1-ken wide, 1-ken high structure with no walls and having the bell at its center (see photo above). Sometimes the four pillars have an inward inclination called {{nihongo|uchikorobi|内転び|lit. inward fall}}. After the Nara period, in which temple layout was rigidly prescribed after the Chinese fashion, the position of the bell tower stopped being prescribed and began to change temple by temple. Roofs are either {{nihongo|gabled|切妻造|[[:en:Gabled roof|kirizuma-zukuri]]}} or {{nihongo|hip-and-gable|入母屋造|{{ill|East Asian hip-and-gable roof|en|East Asian hip-and-gable roof|WD=Q855025|lt=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}}}}. ==Gallery== Chion-in bell 1 by MShades in Kyoto.jpg|Great Bell at Chion-in Gousyouzi 11.jpg|Goshoji-Temple Horyuji-L0337.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Sai-in Shōrō, an example of Nara period bell tower Horyu-ji37s3200.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō, a typical ''hakamagoshi'' type Takidani-hudoumyououji-syourou.jpg|Takidani-hudōmyōō-ji Shōrō Todaiji shoro.jpg|Tōdai-ji's bell tower, an example of the ''fukihanachi'' type, although much larger than the average Komyoji01s3200.jpg|Sometimes the bell is installed in the ''{{ill|Rōmon|en|Rōmon|WD=Q3458957|lt=Rōmon}}''. 崇福寺 (岐阜市)-鐘楼soufukuji007.jpg| Taipei City Tone-Wa Tample Bell tower.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. 臨濟護國禪寺舊鐘樓山門.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]] ==References== {{commons Category|Bell towers in Japan}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} [[en:Shōrō]] {{Commons category|Bell towers in Japan}} [[de:Shōrō]] [[es:Shōrō]] [[eu:Shōrō]] [[fr:Shōrō]] [[id:Shōrō]] [[it:Shōrō]] [[ja:鐘楼]] [[wuu:钟楼（东亚）]] [[zh:鐘樓 (東亞)]] {{wikidata link|Q1363585}} [[Category:Bell towers]] [[Category:Japanese architectural features]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist architecture]] [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shinto architecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

The two main types of bell tower in Japan
The {{nihongo|'''''shōrō''''', '''''shurō'''''|鐘楼|lit. bell building}} or {{nihongo|'''''kanetsuki-[[Dō (architecture)|dō]]'''''|鐘突堂|lit. bell-striking hall}} is the {{ill|Bell tower|en|Bell tower|WD=Q200334|lt=Bell tower}} of a {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|WD=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temple in Japan}}, housing the temple's {{nihongo|''{{ill|Bonshō|en|Bonshō|WD=Q8192099|lt=Bonshō}}''|梵鐘}}. It can also be found at some {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}}s which used to function as temples (see article ''[[Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu shūgō]]''), as for example {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|WD=Q696641|lt=Nikkō Tōshō-gū}}. Two main types exist, the older {{nihongo|''hakamagoshi''|袴腰}}, which has walls, and the more recent {{nihongo|''fukihanachi''|吹放ち}} or {{nihongo|''fukinuki''|吹貫・吹抜き}}, which does not.{{cite book | title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi | editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku | publisher=Shōwa-dō | date=April 10, 1990 | edition=September 30, 2008 | isbn=4-8122-9805-9 | page=82|language=ja}} ==History== During the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}} (710–794), immediately after the arrival of {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|WD=Q736311|lt=Buddhism in Japan}} bell towers were 3 x 2 {{ill|Ken (unit)|en|Ken (unit)|WD=Q3195007|lt=bay}}, 2 storied buildings.{{cite web|title=Shourou|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shourou.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=22 September 2010}} A typical temple ''[[:en:Garan|Garan]]'' had normally two, one to the left and one to the right of the ''[[kyōzō]]'' (or ''kyō-dō''), the {{ill|Sutra|en|Sutra|WD=Q209680|lt=Sutra}} repository. An extant example of this style is [[Hōryū-ji]]'s Sai-in Shōrō in {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara}} (see photo in the gallery). During the following {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}} (794–1185) was developed a new style called ''hakamagoshi'' which consisted of a two storied, {{ill|Hourglass|en|Hourglass|WD=Q179904|lt=hourglass}}-shaped building with the bell hanging from the second story. The earliest extant example is Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō (see photo in the gallery). Finally, during the 13th century the ''fukihanachi'' type was created at [[Tōdai-ji]] by making all structural parts visible. The bell tower in this case usually consists of a 1-ken wide, 1-ken high structure with no walls and having the bell at its center (see photo above). Sometimes the four pillars have an inward inclination called {{nihongo|uchikorobi|内転び|lit. inward fall}}. After the Nara period, in which temple layout was rigidly prescribed after the Chinese fashion, the position of the bell tower stopped being prescribed and began to change temple by temple. Roofs are either {{nihongo|gabled|切妻造|[[:en:Gabled roof|kirizuma-zukuri]]}} or {{nihongo|hip-and-gable|入母屋造|{{ill|East Asian hip-and-gable roof|en|East Asian hip-and-gable roof|WD=Q855025|lt=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}}}}. ==Gallery== Chion-in bell 1 by MShades in Kyoto.jpg|Great Bell at Chion-in Gousyouzi 11.jpg|Goshoji-Temple Horyuji-L0337.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Sai-in Shōrō, an example of Nara period bell tower Horyu-ji37s3200.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō, a typical ''hakamagoshi'' type Takidani-hudoumyououji-syourou.jpg|Takidani-hudōmyōō-ji Shōrō Todaiji shoro.jpg|Tōdai-ji's bell tower, an example of the ''fukihanachi'' type, although much larger than the average Komyoji01s3200.jpg|Sometimes the bell is installed in the ''{{ill|Rōmon|en|Rōmon|WD=Q3458957|lt=Rōmon}}''. 崇福寺 (岐阜市)-鐘楼soufukuji007.jpg| Taipei City Tone-Wa Tample Bell tower.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. 臨濟護國禪寺舊鐘樓山門.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]] ==References== {{commons Category|Bell towers in Japan}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} [[en:Shōrō]] {{Commons category|Bell towers in Japan}} [[de:Shōrō]] [[es:Shōrō]] [[eu:Shōrō]] [[fr:Shōrō]] [[id:Shōrō]] [[it:Shōrō]] [[ja:鐘楼]] [[wuu:钟楼（东亚）]] [[zh:鐘樓 (東亞)]] {{wikidata link|Q1363585}} [[Category:Bell towers]] [[Category:Japanese architectural features]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist architecture]] [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shinto architecture]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

Sometimes the four pillars have an inward inclination called {{nihongo|uchikorobi|内転び|lit. inward fall}}. After the Nara period, in which temple layout was rigidly prescribed after the Chinese fashion, the position of the bell tower stopped being prescribed and began to change temple by temple. Roofs are either {{nihongo|gabled|切妻造|[[:en:Gabled roof|kirizuma-zukuri]]}} or {{nihongo|hip-and-gable|入母屋造|{{ill|East Asian hip-and-gable roof|en|East Asian hip-and-gable roof|WD=Q855025|lt=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}}}}. ==Gallery== Chion-in bell 1 by MShades in Kyoto.jpg|Great Bell at Chion-in Gousyouzi 11.jpg|Goshoji-Temple Horyuji-L0337.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Sai-in Shōrō, an example of Nara period bell tower Horyu-ji37s3200.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō, a typical ''hakamagoshi'' type Takidani-hudoumyououji-syourou.jpg|Takidani-hudōmyōō-ji Shōrō Todaiji shoro.jpg|Tōdai-ji's bell tower, an example of the ''fukihanachi'' type, although much larger than the average Komyoji01s3200.jpg|Sometimes the bell is installed in the ''{{ill|Rōmon|en|Rōmon|WD=Q3458957|lt=Rōmon}}''. 崇福寺 (岐阜市)-鐘楼soufukuji007.jpg| Taipei City Tone-Wa Tample Bell tower.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. 臨濟護國禪寺舊鐘樓山門.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]] ==References== {{commons Category|Bell towers in Japan}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} [[en:Shōrō]] {{Commons category|Bell towers in Japan}} [[de:Shōrō]] [[es:Shōrō]] [[eu:Shōrō]] [[fr:Shōrō]] [[id:Shōrō]] [[it:Shōrō]] [[ja:鐘楼]] [[wuu:钟楼（东亚）]] [[zh:鐘樓 (東亞)]] {{wikidata link|Q1363585}} [[Category:Bell towers]] [[Category:Japanese architectural features]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist architecture]] [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Shinto architecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

revision.text:
[[co:commons:Category:Bell towers in Japan]][[de:Shōrō]][[en:Shōrō]][[es:Shōrō]][[eu:Shōrō]][[fr:Shōrō]][[id:Shōrō]][[it:Shōrō]][[ja:鐘楼]][[wuu:钟楼（东亚）]][[zh:鐘樓 (東亞)]] [[co:commons:Category:Bell towers in Japan]][[de:Shōrō]][[en:Shōrō]][[es:Shōrō]][[eu:Shōrō]][[fr:Shōrō]][[id:Shōrō]][[it:Shōrō]][[ja:鐘楼]][[wuu:钟楼（东亚）]][[zh:鐘樓 (東亞)]] [[sn:commons:Category:Bell towers in Japan]][[de:Shōrō]][[en:Shōrō]][[es:Shōrō]][[eu:Shōrō]][[fr:Shōrō]][[id:Shōrō]][[it:Shōrō]][[ja:鐘楼]][[wuu:钟楼（东亚）]][[zh:鐘樓 (東亞)]] {{Short description|Bell towers in Japan}} {{Italic title|reason=}}
{| style="border-collapse:collapse" cellpadding="0" |style="border:1px solid black;"|[[File:Keiunji Au10 08.JPG|alt=Wooden belltower, old style|x200px]] |style="border:1px solid black;"|[[File:Kencho-ji,-Bell.jpg|alt=Wooden belltower, new style|x200px]] |}
The two main types of bell tower in Japan
The {{nihongo|'''''shōrō''''', '''''shurō'''''|鐘楼|lit. bell building}} or {{nihongo|'''''kanetsuki-[[Dō (architecture)|dō]]'''''|鐘突堂|lit. bell-striking hall}} is the {{ill|Bell tower|en|Bell tower|WD=Q200334|lt=Bell tower}} of a {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|WD=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temple in Japan}}, housing the temple's {{nihongo|''{{ill|Bonshō|en|Bonshō|WD=Q8192099|lt=Bonshō}}''|梵鐘}}. It can also be found at some {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}}s which used to function as temples (see article ''[[Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu shūgō]]''), as for example {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|WD=Q696641|lt=Nikkō Tōshō-gū}}. Two main types exist, the older {{nihongo|''hakamagoshi''|袴腰}}, which has walls, and the more recent {{nihongo|''fukihanachi''|吹放ち}} or {{nihongo|''fukinuki''|吹貫・吹抜き}}, which does not.{{cite book | title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi | editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku | publisher=Shōwa-dō | date=April 10, 1990 | edition=September 30, 2008 | isbn=4-8122-9805-9 | page=82|language=ja}} ==History== During the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}} (710–794), immediately after the arrival of {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|WD=Q736311|lt=Buddhism in Japan}} bell towers were 3 x 2 {{ill|Ken (unit)|en|Ken (unit)|WD=Q3195007|lt=bay}}, 2 storied buildings.{{cite web|title=Shourou|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shourou.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=22 September 2010}} A typical temple ''[[:en:Garan|Garan]]'' had normally two, one to the left and one to the right of the ''[[kyōzō]]'' (or ''kyō-dō''), the {{ill|Sutra|en|Sutra|WD=Q209680|lt=Sutra}} repository. An extant example of this style is [[Hōryū-ji]]'s Sai-in Shōrō in {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara}} (see photo in the gallery). During the following {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}} (794–1185) was developed a new style called ''hakamagoshi'' which consisted of a two storied, {{ill|Hourglass|en|Hourglass|WD=Q179904|lt=hourglass}}-shaped building with the bell hanging from the second story. The earliest extant example is Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō (see photo in the gallery). Finally, during the 13th century the ''fukihanachi'' type was created at [[Tōdai-ji]] by making all structural parts visible. The bell tower in this case usually consists of a 1-ken wide, 1-ken high structure with no walls and having the bell at its center (see photo above). Sometimes the four pillars have an inward inclination called {{nihongo|uchikorobi|内転び|lit. inward fall}}. After the Nara period, in which temple layout was rigidly prescribed after the Chinese fashion, the position of the bell tower stopped being prescribed and began to change temple by temple. Roofs are either {{nihongo|gabled|切妻造|[[:en:Gabled roof|kirizuma-zukuri]]}} or {{nihongo|hip-and-gable|入母屋造|{{ill|East Asian hip-and-gable roof|en|East Asian hip-and-gable roof|WD=Q855025|lt=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}}}}. ==Gallery== Chion-in bell 1 by MShades in Kyoto.jpg|Great Bell at Chion-in Gousyouzi 11.jpg|Goshoji-Temple Horyuji-L0337.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Sai-in Shōrō, an example of Nara period bell tower Horyu-ji37s3200.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō, a typical ''hakamagoshi'' type Takidani-hudoumyououji-syourou.jpg|Takidani-hudōmyōō-ji Shōrō Todaiji shoro.jpg|Tōdai-ji's bell tower, an example of the ''fukihanachi'' type, although much larger than the average Komyoji01s3200.jpg|Sometimes the bell is installed in the ''{{ill|Rōmon|en|Rōmon|WD=Q3458957|lt=Rōmon}}''. 崇福寺 (岐阜市)-鐘楼soufukuji007.jpg| Taipei City Tone-Wa Tample Bell tower.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. 臨濟護國禪寺舊鐘樓山門.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]] ==References== {{commons Category|Bell towers in Japan}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} [[en:Shōrō]] {{Commons category|Bell towers in Japan}} [[de:Shōrō]] [[es:Shōrō]] [[eu:Shōrō]] [[fr:Shōrō]] [[id:Shōrō]] [[it:Shōrō]] [[ja:鐘楼]] [[wuu:钟楼（东亚）]] [[zh:鐘樓 (東亞)]] {{wikidata link|Q1363585}} [[Category:Bell towers]] [[Category:Japanese architectural features]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist architecture]] [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Shinto architecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

The {{nihongo|'''''shōrō''''', '''''shurō'''''|鐘楼|lit. bell building}} or {{nihongo|'''''kanetsuki-[[Dō (architecture)|dō]]'''''|鐘突堂|lit. bell-striking hall}} is the {{ill|Bell tower|en|Bell tower|WD=Q200334|lt=Bell tower|1=Bell tower}} of a {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|WD=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temple in Japan|1=Buddhist temples in Japan}}, housing the temple's {{nihongo|''{{ill|Bonshō|en|Bonshō|WD=Q8192099|lt=Bonshō|1=Bonshō}}''|梵鐘}}. It can also be found at some {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}}s which used to function as temples (see article ''[[Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu shūgō]]''), as for example {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|WD=Q696641|lt=Nikkō Tōshō-gū|1=Nikkō Tōshō-gū}}. Two main types exist, the older {{nihongo|''hakamagoshi''|袴腰}}, which has walls, and the more recent {{nihongo|''fukihanachi''|吹放ち}} or {{nihongo|''fukinuki''|吹貫・吹抜き}}, which does not.{{cite book | title=Nihon Kenchiku-shi | editor=Fujita Masaya, Koga Shūsaku | publisher=Shōwa-dō | date=April 10, 1990 | edition=September 30, 2008 | isbn=4-8122-9805-9 | page=82|language=ja}} ==History== During the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}} (710–794), immediately after the arrival of {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|WD=Q736311|lt=Buddhism in Japan|1=Buddhism in Japan}} bell towers were 3 x 2 {{ill|Ken (unit)|en|Ken (unit)|WD=Q3195007|lt=bay|1=Ken (unit)}}, 2 storied buildings.{{cite web|title=Shourou|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shourou.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=22 September 2010}} A typical temple ''[[:en:Garan|Garan]]'' had normally two, one to the left and one to the right of the ''[[kyōzō]]'' (or ''kyō-dō''), the {{ill|Sutra|en|Sutra|WD=Q209680|lt=Sutra|1=Sutra}} repository. An extant example of this style is [[Hōryū-ji]]'s Sai-in Shōrō in {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}} (see photo in the gallery). During the following {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} (794–1185) was developed a new style called ''hakamagoshi'' which consisted of a two storied, {{ill|Hourglass|en|Hourglass|WD=Q179904|lt=hourglass|1=Hourglass}}-shaped building with the bell hanging from the second story. The earliest extant example is Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō (see photo in the gallery). Finally, during the 13th century the ''fukihanachi'' type was created at [[Tōdai-ji]] by making all structural parts visible. The bell tower in this case usually consists of a 1-ken wide, 1-ken high structure with no walls and having the bell at its center (see photo above). Sometimes the four pillars have an inward inclination called {{nihongo|uchikorobi|内転び|lit. inward fall}}. After the Nara period, in which temple layout was rigidly prescribed after the Chinese fashion, the position of the bell tower stopped being prescribed and began to change temple by temple. Roofs are either {{nihongo|gabled|切妻造|[[:en:Gabled roof|kirizuma-zukuri]]}} or {{nihongo|hip-and-gable|入母屋造|{{ill|East Asian hip-and-gable roof|en|East Asian hip-and-gable roof|WD=Q855025|lt=East Asian hip-and-gable roof|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}}}}. ==Gallery== Chion-in bell 1 by MShades in Kyoto.jpg|Great Bell at Chion-in Gousyouzi 11.jpg|Goshoji-Temple Horyuji-L0337.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Sai-in Shōrō, an example of Nara period bell tower Horyu-ji37s3200.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō, a typical ''hakamagoshi'' type Takidani-hudoumyououji-syourou.jpg|Takidani-hudōmyōō-ji Shōrō Todaiji shoro.jpg|Tōdai-ji's bell tower, an example of the ''fukihanachi'' type, although much larger than the average Komyoji01s3200.jpg|Sometimes the bell is installed in the ''{{ill|Rōmon|en|Rōmon|WD=Q3458957|lt=Rōmon|1=Rōmon}}''. 崇福寺 (岐阜市)-鐘楼soufukuji007.jpg| Taipei City Tone-Wa Tample Bell tower.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. 臨濟護國禪寺舊鐘樓山門.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]] ==References== {{commons Category|Bell towers in Japan}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} [[en:Shōrō]] {{Commons category|Bell towers in Japan}} [[de:Shōrō]] [[es:Shōrō]] [[eu:Shōrō]] [[fr:Shōrō]] [[id:Shōrō]] [[it:Shōrō]] [[ja:鐘楼]] [[wuu:钟楼（东亚）]] [[zh:鐘樓 (東亞)]] {{wikidata link|Q1363585}} [[Category:Bell towers]] [[Category:Japanese architectural features]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist architecture]] [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Shinto architecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages]]

{| style="border-collapse:collapse" cellpadding="0" |style="border:1px solid black;"|[[File:Keiunji Au10 08.JPG|alt=Wooden belltower, old style|x200px]] |style="border:1px solid black;"|[[File:Kencho-ji,-Bell.jpg|alt=Wooden belltower, new style|x200px]] |}

Sometimes the four pillars have an inward inclination called {{nihongo|uchikorobi|内転び|lit. inward fall}}. After the Nara period, in which temple layout was rigidly prescribed after the Chinese fashion, the position of the bell tower stopped being prescribed and began to change temple by temple. Roofs are either {{nihongo|gabled|切妻造|[[:en:Gabled roof|kirizuma-zukuri]]}} or {{nihongo|hip-and-gable|入母屋造|{{ill|East Asian hip-and-gable roof|en|East Asian hip-and-gable roof|WD=Q855025|lt=East Asian hip-and-gable roof|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}}}}. ==Gallery== Chion-in bell 1 by MShades in Kyoto.jpg|Great Bell at Chion-in Gousyouzi 11.jpg|Goshoji-Temple Horyuji-L0337.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Sai-in Shōrō, an example of Nara period bell tower Horyu-ji37s3200.jpg|Hōryū-ji's Tō-in Shōrō, a typical ''hakamagoshi'' type Takidani-hudoumyououji-syourou.jpg|Takidani-hudōmyōō-ji Shōrō Todaiji shoro.jpg|Tōdai-ji's bell tower, an example of the ''fukihanachi'' type, although much larger than the average Komyoji01s3200.jpg|Sometimes the bell is installed in the ''{{ill|Rōmon|en|Rōmon|WD=Q3458957|lt=Rōmon|1=Rōmon}}''. 崇福寺 (岐阜市)-鐘楼soufukuji007.jpg| Taipei City Tone-Wa Tample Bell tower.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. 臨濟護國禪寺舊鐘樓山門.jpg|''shōrō'' in Taipei, Taiwan. ==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]] ==References== {{commons Category|Bell towers in Japan}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} [[en:Shōrō]] {{Commons category|Bell towers in Japan}} [[de:Shōrō]] [[es:Shōrō]] [[eu:Shōrō]] [[fr:Shōrō]] [[id:Shōrō]] [[it:Shōrō]] [[ja:鐘楼]] [[wuu:钟楼（东亚）]] [[zh:鐘樓 (東亞)]] {{wikidata link|Q1363585}} [[Category:Bell towers]] [[Category:Japanese architectural features]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist architecture]] [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Shinto architecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
